summary: Parallel deep-read of every Mindline subsystem with structured critique

issues.title: "E2E encryption" does not exist: no key exchange, chat messages broadcast in plaintext
issues.detail: Room keys are generated per-browser (crypto.rs:70-91 via crypto.getRandomValues) and never leave the device — there is no key-exchange mechanism anywhere in the codebase. The JS layer admits this: src/lib/p2p/handlers.ts:203-204 "peers may have different room keys (key sharing not yet implemented)" and deliberately sends sync responses with encrypted:false. The send path proves it: src/routes/[roomId]/+page.svelte:153-172 calls wasm.sendMessageEnhanced(roomId, content, ...) with plaintext, then broadcastChat(content, messageId); src/lib/p2p/manager.ts:254-262 builds the ChatMessage with raw content and never sets encrypted:true. encrypt_message_content (crypto_api.rs:76) has ZERO call sites in the entire app (verified by grep). The only encryption protecting messages is WebRTC's built-in DTLS transport encryption plus the TS-only relay fallback. Meanwhile README.md:24,30,222,334 claims "End-to-End Encryption ... All messages encrypted with 256-bit keys" — this is false advertising for a privacy product.

issues.title: Decrypt path has a latent type bug proving it never ran: Promise assigned as message content
issues.detail: decrypt_message_content is an async Rust export returning js_sys::Promise (crypto_api.rs:97-111, future_to_promise). The TS proxy types it as a synchronous string function (src/lib/wasm/index.ts:291-292), and handlers.ts:98-107 calls it synchronously: `const result = wasm.decryptMessageContent(content); if (result) decryptedContent = result;`. A Promise is always truthy, so if any peer ever set encrypted:true, the rendered message content would become a Promise object ("[object Promise]"). Same bug at handlers.ts:253 in sync-response handling. Today this path is dead (no sender sets the flag), but it demonstrates the encryption pipeline has never been exercised end-to-end and will break the moment someone "turns it on".

issues.title: Room keys and full message history stored as plaintext JSON in localStorage
issues.detail: save_room_key_to_storage (crypto.rs:243-265) serializes the raw 32-byte key as a JSON byte array into localStorage key `mindline_encryption_key_{roomId}`; export_key (crypto.rs:189-196) also emits plaintext JSON. Worse, the entire chat history including plaintext content is persisted via save_room_to_storage (messages.rs:524-580) into `chatHistory_{roomId}` — so even if wire encryption existed, at-rest data is fully exposed to any XSS, malicious extension, or device access. There is no encryption at rest despite README security claims. Tamper-resistance is similarly hollow: the "integrity_hash" (crypto.rs:128-129, verified at 147-156 and 398-411) is an unkeyed SHA-256 of ciphertext+nonce stored in the same blob — any attacker can recompute it; it adds zero security over GCM's auth tag and the code comment admits it.

issues.title: Roughly two-thirds of the Rust code is dead or ceremonial at runtime
issues.detail: Verified by grepping all wasm.* call sites: only ~15 exports are ever called. Completely dead subsystems: logger.rs (701 lines) + logging_api.rs (220) — zero JS call sites; performance.rs (343) + bindings — zero call sites, frame rate hardcoded to 60.0 (performance.rs:289) and CPU usage is a fabricated estimate (292-298); storage.rs (154) — the "IndexedDBManager" is an in-memory HashMap with a comment admitting "In production, this would use IndexedDB" (storage.rs:42-43), zero call sites, while Cargo.toml:37-46 pulls 10 unused Idb* web-sys features; sanitizer.rs (283) + validation_api.rs — zero call sites, and its detect_sql_injection (sanitizer.rs:209-221) would flag any chat message containing "select", an apostrophe, or a semicolon in a database-free P2P app; legacy core.rs send_message/get_messages/join_room — unused; create_room_with_id IS called at runtime but is a pure no-op that logs (core.rs:245-253); state_api.rs draft/peer tracking — unused, duplicated by Svelte stores; utils.rs URL helpers — unused (SvelteKit routing handles this). The base64 crate (Cargo.toml:58) and Rtc* web-sys features (Cargo.toml:29-36) are entirely unused — p2p.rs no longer exists despite CLAUDE.md:55 still documenting it.

issues.title: 1.05 MB WASM payload (293 KB brotli / 410 KB gzip) for functionality that is mostly dead weight
issues.detail: Measured: mindline_bg.wasm is 1,080,091 bytes raw, 410,242 gzip, 293,022 brotli — even with opt-level="z", LTO, and panic=abort already applied (Cargo.toml:62-66). This is the largest single asset in a mobile-first chat app and must download+instantiate before the room page works (wasm.generateUuid/initialize are on the join path). Major size contributors are the regex crate (used only by the dead sanitizer), serde_json/serde-wasm-bindgen (every message crosses the boundary twice through serde), uuid, and sha2 — while the actual cryptography executes in JavaScript. The payload buys almost nothing the browser doesn't already provide.

issues.title: Message state lives in three places with hand-rolled synchronization
issues.detail: Every message is dual-written: Svelte messages store (handlers.ts:134) AND WASM MessageManager (handlers.ts:140 receiveMessageFromPeer) AND localStorage (handlers.ts:142 saveRoomMessagesToStorage). Edits/deletes/reactions repeat this triple-write pattern (handlers.ts:383-400, 423-438, 480-496). The WASM side additionally keeps a parallel legacy copy in APP_STATE (core.rs:111-162) and a multi-tab merge-on-save (messages.rs:534-569) that re-reads and re-merges localStorage on every save — O(n) JSON parse+serialize of the whole room per message. Any drift bug between the three stores produces ghost/duplicate messages; the deduplication logic exists precisely because the architecture invites divergence.

issues.title: Mutex/Arc ceremony, no-op catch_unwind, and panic sites
issues.detail: WASM here is single-threaded, yet state.rs:8-15 and messages.rs:627-629 wrap globals in thread_local!{Arc<Mutex<...>>} — triple redundancy (thread_local alone isolates; Arc is never shared; Mutex can never contend). crypto.rs:324, sanitizer.rs:268, logger.rs:650 inconsistently use RefCell for the same purpose. All ~20 "Failed to lock" error paths are unreachable: with panic="abort" (Cargo.toml:66) Mutex poisoning cannot occur. The std::panic::catch_unwind guards in logging_api.rs:54-88 are no-ops under panic=abort — a panic still kills the whole WASM instance. Real panic risks: logger.rs:625 web_sys::window().unwrap() aborts in any non-window context, and futures::executor::block_on (advanced_api.rs:52,65) would hang the main thread if those storage futures ever did real async I/O (currently saved only by the futures being secretly synchronous, and by having zero call sites). message_api.rs:56-65 works around a wasm-bindgen Option<u32> sentinel (0x100000001) with a magic "> 1000000" check — evidence of fragile FFI.

issues.title: Relay-fallback E2E (the only real working E2E) is unauthenticated ECDH, MITM-able by the signaling server
issues.detail: Noted for completeness since it is the only genuine end-to-end crypto in the product and it lives entirely in TypeScript, not WASM: connection.ts:111-250 does ECDH P-256 + AES-GCM per peer for the WebSocket relay path. Public keys are exchanged through the signaling server with no authentication or fingerprint verification, so a malicious server can substitute keys and read relayed traffic. It is nonetheless a working template for the missing room-level key exchange — and proof the team can ship WebCrypto-only crypto without Rust.

issues.title: CLAUDE.md / docs describe an architecture that no longer exists
issues.detail: CLAUDE.md:54-58 lists p2p.rs (deleted — lib.rs:18 says "all P2P coordination now in JavaScript"), claims "Enter key → WASM encrypts message → P2P broadcast" (false: broadcast is plaintext), and "Messages encrypted with AES-256-GCM, per-room keys" (false at runtime). The mission-critical data-flow documentation actively misleads anyone working on the code.

opportunities: Delete the Rust/WASM layer entirely. The live surface — ordered message list with dedup, edit/delete/reactions, localStorage persistence, UUID generation — is ~200-300 lines of TypeScript, half of which already exists in the Svelte stores (the app dual-writes today). This removes ~4,400 lines of Rust, the 360-line proxy (src/lib/wasm/index.ts), the wasm-pack build pipeline, and 293-410 KB of compressed payload from the mobile critical path, with zero security loss because all actual cryptography already executes in WebCrypto via the JS bridge (src/lib/wasm/crypto.ts).

Implement real key exchange to make the E2E claim true. Cheapest robust option for this product: put the room key in the URL fragment (#k=...) at room creation — fragments never reach the signaling server — and encrypt all chat/sync payloads with it via WebCrypto AES-GCM (the primitives and 12-byte-random-IV pattern already exist and are correct). Alternative: extend the existing per-peer ECDH relay handshake (connection.ts:111-250) to cover DataChannel payloads, adding key fingerprint verification (short emoji/word fingerprint in UI) to close the MITM hole. This also gives the planned P2P file/photo/audio/video sharing a real encryption story for free.
Encrypt-at-rest or stop persisting plaintext: derive a storage key from the room key and encrypt chatHistory_* blobs, or at minimum remove the README e2e/security claims until true.

Collapse message state to one owner: a single TypeScript message store that the UI subscribes to and that persists itself, eliminating the triple-write (Svelte store + WASM MessageManager + localStorage) and the hand-rolled multi-tab merge.

If keeping any Rust: strip logger.rs, performance.rs, storage.rs, sanitizer.rs, legacy core.rs functions, state_api drafts/peers, and unused deps (regex, base64, Idb*/Rtc* web-sys features) — this alone should roughly halve the WASM binary; then fix the async decrypt proxy typing. But given the live remainder is a message list, deletion beats dieting.
Update CLAUDE.md/README to match reality (p2p.rs gone, no wire encryption today) so future agents and contributors don't build on false assumptions.
strengths: messages.rs core logic is genuinely solid: chronological insertion (messages.rs:255-280), HashSet-based deduplication, multi-tab merge-on-save with in-memory precedence (messages.rs:524-580), and a 1000-message cache cap — backed by 19 passing unit tests (the only tested code in the crate; verified with cargo test).

The cryptographic primitives chosen are correct: AES-256-GCM with 128-bit tag, fresh 12-byte random IV per encryption from crypto.getRandomValues, PBKDF2-SHA256 at 600k iterations available in the bridge (src/lib/wasm/crypto.ts), and 32-byte CSPRNG room keys. Nothing home-rolled — when/if invoked, the crypto itself is sound.
The safe WASM proxy pattern (src/lib/wasm/index.ts createSafeCall) with parameter validation, NaN guards, and descriptive errors is well-engineered defensive FFI.
Live API functions consistently avoid unwrap/panic in favor of Result<_, JsValue> with descriptive messages; the no-unwrap-in-production convention is actually followed in the runtime paths.

The codebase is honest in comments where it matters: handlers.ts:203-204 admits key sharing is unimplemented, storage.rs:42-43 admits the IndexedDB manager is fake — the dead code is at least labeled, which made this audit tractable.
result.summary:
The Rust/WASM core (src-rust/, ~4,400 lines, compiled to a 1.05 MB / 293 KB-brotli cdylib) presents itself as the security and state engine of Mindline: AES-256-GCM encryption (crypto.rs), message management (messages.rs), input sanitization (sanitizer.rs), logging (logger.rs), performance monitoring (performance.rs), and IndexedDB storage (storage.rs), exposed through six *_api.rs binding modules and consumed via a typed proxy in src/lib/wasm/index.ts. There is no p2p.rs — P2P moved entirely to TypeScript (lib.rs:18), though CLAUDE.md still documents it.

At runtime the picture is very different. Tracing every wasm.* call site in the app shows only ~15 exports are ever invoked, almost all from messages.rs: send_message_enhanced, receive_message_from_peer, get_room_messages, edit/delete/reactions, and plaintext save/load to localStorage. The logging system (921 lines), performance monitor (343 lines, with a hardcoded 60.0 FPS and fabricated CPU metric), the fake \"IndexedDBManager\" (an in-memory HashMap that admits in comments it isn't IndexedDB), the sanitizer/validation API, the legacy core.rs functions, and the draft/peer state tracking all have zero JavaScript call sites — they exist only to inflate the binary, much of whose size comes from the regex crate that only the dead sanitizer uses.

The encryption story is the critical finding. Room keys are real 32-byte CSPRNG keys, generated per-browser on room join (initialize_room_encryption, crypto_api.rs:43) and stored as plaintext JSON in localStorage — but there is no key exchange of any kind, so no two peers ever share a key. Consequently the app never encrypts anything: encrypt_message_content has zero call sites, broadcastChat (manager.ts:239-265) sends raw plaintext over the DataChannel, sync responses are deliberately unencrypted (handlers.ts:203-204, \"key sharing not yet implemented\"), and chat history is persisted as plaintext JSON. The decrypt path that does exist is dead (the encrypted flag is never set) and contains a latent bug — a Promise-returning async export consumed synchronously (handlers.ts:100), which would render \"[object Promise]\" as message content if ever triggered. Even the Rust \"crypto\" is a pass-through: actual AES-GCM runs in JavaScript via a window.webcrypto* bridge (src/lib/wasm/crypto.ts), with Rust adding only serialization overhead and an unkeyed SHA-256 \"integrity hash\" that provides no security. The only genuine end-to-end encryption in the product is the WebSocket relay fallback — unauthenticated ECDH P-256 + AES-GCM implemented entirely in TypeScript (connection.ts:111-250), with WASM uninvolved. Message security on the direct path rests solely on WebRTC's built-in DTLS. Thread-safety code is ceremony: thread_local!{Arc<Mutex<>>} globals in a single-threaded WASM environment, unreachable lock-poisoning error paths (panic=abort), no-op catch_unwind guards, plus a window().unwrap() abort risk and a block_on footgun in dead code paths.
result.verdict: Rewrite by deletion. The Rust/WASM layer is not pulling its weight: its cryptography is performed by JavaScript WebCrypto anyway (Rust just ferries bytes across the FFI boundary), roughly two-thirds of its code is dead at runtime, and its one live contribution — an ordered, deduplicated message store with localStorage persistence — is a few hundred lines of TypeScript that the Svelte stores already half-duplicate. Removing it cuts 293-410 KB of compressed payload from the mobile critical path, eliminates the wasm-pack toolchain and the fragile triple-state synchronization, and loses nothing security-wise because there is currently no message encryption to lose: no key exchange exists, messages broadcast and persist in plaintext, and the README's E2E claims are false. The engineering effort should go where the product gap actually is — a real key-exchange design (URL-fragment room key or authenticated per-peer ECDH, for which the TypeScript relay code is already a working template) implemented directly on WebCrypto, which will also serve the planned P2P file/photo/audio/video features. WASM would only earn its place back if the roadmap later demands heavy client-side compute (e.g., media transcoding or chunked file crypto at throughput), and even then as a small purpose-built module, not this one.

result.summary:
The P2P layer is a hand-rolled WebRTC mesh in three pieces: `connection.ts` (1511 lines) owns the signaling WebSocket, RTCPeerConnection lifecycle, a single 'chat' DataChannel per peer, an encrypted WebSocket relay fallback (ECDH P-256 → AES-GCM), and mesh-repair timers; `manager.ts` is a module-singleton orchestrator wiring callbacks into Svelte stores plus reconnection backoff and mobile lifecycle (visibility/network/pagehide) handlers; `handlers.ts` routes 9 message types (chat/typing/sync/edit/delete/reaction/ack/user-connected/ready) into stores and WASM persistence. Discovery flows through `signaling-server.js` (a solid, rate-limited ws server that assigns server-side client IDs and relays offer/answer/ICE/relay-key/relay to targeted peers). Initiator selection is deterministic (higher server ID offers), with offer-collision rollback, queued-offer retry, ICE candidate queueing, and a ready-handshake before `onPeerConnected` fires.

The realtime typing feature is full-snapshot based: every input event is trailing-debounced 100ms (`+page.svelte:182-193`), then `broadcastTyping` sends the entire draft text as a JSON 'typing' message to all peers; receivers store it in a drafts map keyed by peerId with a 3s fade-out. Because each update is a complete snapshot over an ordered channel, packet loss/reorder self-heals on the next keystroke — the protocol itself is sound. The problem is underneath it: the shared DataChannel is created with `{ ordered: true, maxRetransmits: 3 }` (connection.ts:627-630), which makes it *partially reliable* — chat messages, edits, acks and sync responses share a channel that silently drops messages after 3 retransmits on lossy networks, and high-frequency typing traffic head-of-line-blocks chat on that same ordered channel.

Sync-on-reconnect is naive: the requester broadcasts a `sync-request` carrying `lastSync` and `messageCount` (manager.ts:366-393), but responders ignore both and ship the *entire* room history as one JSON DataChannel message (handlers.ts:192-220). Since `requestSync` is broadcast and every peer answers, an N-peer room produces N full-history responses per join (O(N²) data), and a long history will exceed cross-browser SCTP message limits (~64-256KB) and silently fail. Conflict handling is dedupe-by-id, deletion-wins, and a shallow reaction merge that resurrects removed reactions; edits are never reconciled. Notably, chat broadcast and sync both travel as plaintext JSON — `encrypted: true` is never set anywhere in the codebase — so the WASM AES-GCM room key only encrypts localStorage; on-the-wire confidentiality rests entirely on DTLS plus an unauthenticated ECDH for the relay path (the signaling server could MITM relay-key exchange since it assigns `fromId`).

Failure recovery, the part that matters most for "ultra-reliable", is the weakest area. Several recovery paths are provably dead code: `restartIce()` is called (connection.ts:754, 1392) but no `onnegotiationneeded` handler or manual re-offer exists, so ICE restart never actually renegotiates; the relay-fallback-after-ICE-exhaustion path adds the peer to `relayPeers` *after* `removePeer()` already wiped it from `allKnownPeers` (761-772 vs 1304), and the relay loop requires `allKnownPeers.has(peerId)` (1168), so it never fires; and any `connectionState === 'failed'` instantly calls `removePeer` (696-699), which deletes the peer from `allKnownPeers`, removing it from mesh monitoring forever — a single post-handshake failure permanently partitions two peers until someone refreshes the page. A signaling WebSocket blip also tears down every healthy DataChannel (380-392) even though they don't depend on signaling.
strengths: Deterministic initiator rule using server-assigned IDs (connection.ts:428-434, 468-478) plus offer-collision rollback with retry queue (956-1019) — the hardest WebRTC race is genuinely handled
ICE candidates queued until remote description is set, then flushed (connection.ts:1093-1132) — correct and common source of bugs elsewhere
Ready-handshake over the DataChannel before firing onPeerConnected (786-898) prevents sending into half-open channels, including the already-open-on-ondatachannel edge case (846-850)

Encrypted WebSocket relay fallback (ECDH P-256 + AES-GCM, connection.ts:111-274) so the signaling server cannot passively read fallback traffic, with pending-message queueing until key exchange completes

signaling-server.js is genuinely production-shaped: server-assigned client IDs prevent fromId spoofing (line 196, 340), per-client/IP/room rate limits, keepalive with mobile-tolerant missed-pong allowance (447-471), room/connection caps, targeted (not broadcast) signaling relay

Mobile lifecycle awareness exists and is mostly sensible: pagehide/beforeunload cleanup, visibility handler that checks actual WS state before reconnecting (manager.ts:531-568), 'disconnected' treated as transient rather than terminal (connection.ts:704-710)
Reconnection with exponential backoff surfaces state to the UI (manager.ts:418-475) and is guarded against firing during intentional disconnects
removePeer nulls handlers before close and dedupes via disconnectingPeers (1261-1323) — listener cleanup on the happy path is thorough

issues.title: Any post-handshake connection failure permanently severs the peer
issues.detail: removePeer() deletes the peer from allKnownPeers. It is called on connectionState 'failed' (connection.ts:696-699), data-channel death, and ICE exhaustion — i.e. on transient failures, not just peer-left. Once removed, the mesh monitor (1419-1469 iterates allKnownPeers), ensureFullMesh, and both relay loops (1167-1184) all forget the peer exists. There is no signaling re-announcement for peers that never left the room, so two peers whose connection drops (WiFi blip, NAT rebind) stay partitioned until one refreshes the page. The design conflates 'peer left the room' with 'connection to peer failed'.

issues.title: Relay fallback after ICE exhaustion is dead code (ordering bug)
issues.detail: On ICE restart exhaustion: this.removePeer(peerId) runs at line 766 (wiping allKnownPeers at 1304 and relayPeers at 1300), then this.relayPeers.add(peerId) at 770. broadcast()'s relay path requires `this.allKnownPeers.has(peerId)` (line 1168), which is now false, and the second relay loop (1175-1184) also iterates allKnownPeers. The advertised 'encrypted WebSocket relay when direct P2P fails' never activates via this path — verified by tracing both relay loops.

issues.title: restartIce() recovery is a no-op — no renegotiation ever happens
issues.detail: pc.restartIce() only flags the next createOffer() to generate fresh ICE credentials; without an onnegotiationneeded handler or a manual offer/answer round, nothing is sent. grep confirms no 'onnegotiationneeded' anywhere in src/lib/p2p/. Both call sites (connection.ts:754 and 1392) therefore do nothing. Worse, the exponential-backoff ICE retry races against onconnectionstatechange 'failed' (696-699) which calls removePeer immediately, so the backoff timers usually fire against an already-removed peer. The entire ICE-restart subsystem (iceRestartAttempts, MAX_ICE_RESTARTS, handleConnectionFailure) is effectively inert.

issues.title: DataChannel is partially reliable: chat messages can be silently dropped
issues.detail: createDataChannel('chat', { ordered: true, maxRetransmits: 3 }) creates a partially-reliable channel: any message (chat, edit, delete, sync-response, delivery-ack) is abandoned after 3 retransmission attempts. On lossy mobile networks this silently loses chat messages with no error and no retry — the delivery-ack system will show non-delivery but nothing re-sends. A chat app's primary channel must be fully reliable (omit maxRetransmits). This also disqualifies the channel for file chunking as-is.

issues.title: Signaling WebSocket close tears down all healthy P2P connections
issues.detail: ws.onclose iterates this.peers and removePeer()s every one, even though established DataChannels do not depend on the signaling server. A signaling blip (server redeploy, fly.dev restart, mobile radio handoff) kills every healthy peer connection and forces full re-mesh through the staggered-delay join flow. Peers should survive signaling loss; signaling is only needed for new negotiations.

issues.title: No teardown guard: timers resurrect connections after disconnect()
issues.detail: P2PConnection has no 'destroyed' flag. After disconnect(), already-scheduled timers still fire: the staggered room-joined connects (439-453, up to peers*2s out), the offer-timeout retry which unconditionally calls createPeerConnection(peerId, true) (673-680), ICE backoff timers (751-760), and queued-offer retries (1012, 1077). Each creates a fresh RTCPeerConnection in the orphaned instance's peers map that nothing ever closes — a real leak when switching rooms or during manager-driven reconnects, where manager.ts nulls its reference (manager.ts:168) but the instance keeps working in the background.

issues.title: Sync sends full history as one giant JSON message, ignores lastSync, and floods O(N²)
issues.detail: handleSyncRequest ignores message.lastSync and message.messageCount and sends messages.getRoomMessages(roomId) — the whole room, unpaginated — as a single JSON string via sendToPeer. Cross-browser SCTP message limits (~64KB safe, ~256KB Chrome-Chrome) mean long histories throw on send and sync silently fails (sendToPeer just logs, connection.ts:1238-1240). And because requestSync is *broadcast* (manager.ts:388) on every onPeerConnected (manager.ts:97), every peer in an N-room answers with full history: N joins produce N² full-history transfers.

issues.title: Double onPeerDisconnected on connection failure corrupts delivery stats and double-toasts
issues.detail: In onconnectionstatechange 'failed': removePeer(peerId) fires onPeerDisconnectedCallback once (1314), then lines 701-703 fire it again explicitly. The disconnectingPeers dedup only guards removePeer itself. Manager's handler then runs twice: two '<name> left the room' toasts, and delivery.handlePeerDisconnect double-decrements totalPeers for every undelivered message (delivery.ts:68-79 — deliveredTo still lacks the peer on the second call), skewing read-receipt UI.

issues.title: TURN strategy: discontinued free openrelay + forceRelay funnels all cellular traffic through it
issues.detail: The only TURN servers are openrelay.metered.ca free-tier with public static credentials — a best-effort shared service with no SLA (and widely reported as overloaded/retired). getP2PConfig sets forceRelay: mobileNetwork (config.ts:154), which becomes iceTransportPolicy: 'relay' (connection.ts:313): on cellular, *no* candidates except TURN are even tried, so if openrelay is down or throttled, mobile users get zero connectivity — strictly worse than letting ICE try srflx first. Cross-NAT reliability for a production app requires self-hosted coturn (or paid TURN) with ephemeral HMAC credentials. STUN config (4 Google servers) is fine; iceCandidatePoolSize 10/20 is wasteful but harmless.

issues.title: No backpressure, chunking, or binary path — not ready for file/media transfer
issues.detail: broadcast() and sendToPeer() (1230-1246) call channel.send() blind: no bufferedAmount check, no bufferedAmountLowThreshold/onbufferedamountlow anywhere (grep confirms), no chunk protocol, JSON-string-only framing (binary would need base64, +33% size). binaryType='arraybuffer' is set (879) but nothing ever sends or receives binary. Combined with maxRetransmits:3 dropping frames under congestion, sending a photo today would both stall the typing/chat channel and lose chunks. File/media support needs: a dedicated reliable channel per transfer, 16KB binary chunks, backpressure via bufferedAmountLowThreshold, and a transfer manifest/resume protocol — none of which exists to build on.

issues.title: Plaintext chat on the wire: E2E room encryption never applied to P2P transport
issues.detail: broadcastChat builds ChatMessage with raw content and never sets encrypted: true (grep over src finds zero occurrences). handleSyncRequest explicitly sends encrypted: false with a comment admitting room-key sharing 'not yet implemented' (handlers.ts:203-211). So the AES-256-GCM room key only encrypts localStorage; wire confidentiality is DTLS (fine against outsiders) plus, for relay fallback, an *unauthenticated* ECDH (connection.ts:167-198) where the signaling server assigns fromId and forwards public keys — i.e. the server can MITM the relay key exchange. The 'P2P E2E-encrypted' claim is not what the transport implements; for the product this is a trust-model gap, not just a nit.

issues.title: 16KB signaling maxPayload vs unbounded chat content: one long message can kill the signaling connection
issues.detail: ws maxPayload is 16KB; exceeding it closes the WebSocket (1009). Chat content has no length cap (MessageInput.svelte has no maxlength; verified by grep), and a long message broadcast to a relay-fallback peer is AES-GCM-encrypted + base64'd (+33%) into a relay frame. Result: ~11KB of text nukes the signaling connection, which (per the critical issue above) tears down all peers. Also, the client's handleSignalingMessage switch (connection.ts:402-567) has no 'error' case, so server rate-limit and validation errors are silently swallowed.

issues.title: Slow mesh formation: staggered 2s-per-peer connects
issues.detail: On room-joined, connection attempts are staggered i*2000ms + (charCode % 1000). Joining a 5-peer room means the last peer connection doesn't even *start* for ~9 seconds, plus ensureFullMesh waits (peers+1)*1000ms (460-462). For a product whose pitch is instant presence, multi-second join latency is self-inflicted; the deterministic initiator rule already prevents glare, so the stagger mostly adds delay.

issues.title: Sync conflict handling: edits never reconciled, reaction removals resurrected
issues.detail: For messages that already exist locally, sync only applies deletion-wins and a reaction merge. Edited content is ignored — if a peer edited a message while you were offline, sync will not deliver the edit (the edit broadcast was lost forever). The reaction merge `{...existing, ...synced}` is a per-emoji overwrite, not a union, and a peer whose state predates a reaction *removal* will re-add it on every sync. There are no version/lamport stamps to converge on; multi-device divergence is permanent.

issues.title: Mesh retry accounting is incoherent: cap defeated or instant permanent abandonment
issues.detail: meshConnectionAttempts caps retries at 3, but removePeer deletes the counter (1307) AND the allKnownPeers entry (1304). So either a failure goes through removePeer (peer vanishes from mesh iteration entirely — see critical issue) or it doesn't (stuck 'new' state) and after 3 attempts the peer is skipped forever since the counter only resets on success (1432). Also createPeerConnection at 1467 and 1492 is an unawaited floating promise — rejections become unhandled.

issues.title: Network-change handler hard-reconnects on every navigator.connection 'change' event
issues.detail: The handler does not check whether anything actually broke — it always tears down and rejoins. Chrome fires 'change' on effectiveType/downlink estimate fluctuations, which happen routinely on cellular, so mobile users get periodic full teardowns ('Network changed, reconnecting...' toast) of perfectly healthy connections. Should check isWebSocketConnected/getConnectedPeers first like the visibility handler does (541-547).

issues.title: Typing debounce is trailing-only and shares the lossy ordered channel
issues.detail: handleTyping clears and re-arms a 100ms timer per keystroke, so sustained typing faster than one key/100ms suppresses all intermediate broadcasts until a pause — the 'see each character' effect degrades exactly when typing is most lively (a throttle with trailing edge would fix it). Snapshots self-heal loss, which is the right design, but they ride the same ordered maxRetransmits:3 channel as chat: typing bursts can HOL-block chat, and dropped final snapshots leave stale drafts until the 3s timeout (drafts.ts:16). handleTypingMessage also ignores message.timestamp (handlers.ts:170-187), so a late relay-path snapshot can overwrite a newer one when delivery migrates between channel and relay.

issues.title: broadcast() return count lies; delivery tracking disagrees with it
issues.detail: Relay sends increment successCount even when sendRelay merely queues the message pending key exchange (1204-1210) or silently returns because the WS is closed (1202). Meanwhile delivery.trackMessage only counts open-DataChannel peers (manager.ts:250-253). So broadcastMessage()'s boolean and the delivery UI can both misreport. iceRestartAttempts is also never cleaned in removePeer (small per-peer map leak), and onicegatheringstatechange is not nulled there (1286-1296).

issues.title: Desktop gets no visibility/network recovery handlers; config knobs are dead
issues.detail: setupVisibilityHandler/setupNetworkHandler are registered only when isMobileDevice() — desktop laptop sleep/wake relies solely on ws.onclose firing. Both setup functions also ignore their roomId/config params (manager.ts:523, 588). config.maxReconnectAttempts (mobile 10) is never read — manager hardcodes MAX_RECONNECT_ATTEMPTS=7 (manager.ts:19). connectionTimeout defaults to an aggressive 2000ms on desktop (types.ts:36, config.ts:152), enough to spuriously fall into 'local mode' on a cold cross-region WSS handshake.

opportunities: Adopt the standard 'perfect negotiation' pattern (onnegotiationneeded + polite/impolite peer based on the existing server-ID rule). It replaces the negotiatingPeers set, pendingOffers queue, collision retry/backoff, offer timeouts, and manual rollback logic — roughly 400 lines of connection.ts — with ~50 lines that also make restartIce() actually work.

Split traffic across two DataChannels: one fully reliable ordered channel (no maxRetransmits) for chat/sync/edits/acks, and one unordered+maxRetransmits:0 channel for typing snapshots. This fixes silent chat loss, removes HOL blocking, and makes the typing feature smoother for free.

Separate 'peer left room' from 'connection failed': keep allKnownPeers authoritative from signaling events only, and let a single reconnection loop (mesh monitor) own all retry logic. This one change fixes the permanent-partition bug, the dead relay fallback, and the incoherent retry caps simultaneously.

Make sync delta-based: honor the lastSync field that is already sent, respond only to the requester (sendToPeer, not broadcast-and-everyone-answers), paginate responses into <16KB chunks. This is a prerequisite for rooms with real history and costs little.

For the planned file/photo/audio/video feature: build a new transfer module rather than extending broadcast() — dedicated reliable DataChannel per transfer, binary 16KB chunks with bufferedAmountLowThreshold backpressure, manifest + resume offsets. Nothing in the current send path is reusable for this.

Replace openrelay with self-hosted coturn (fly.io/Railway, ~$5/mo) using ephemeral HMAC credentials fetched from the signaling server; drop forceRelay/iceTransportPolicy:'relay' and let ICE pick the best path on cellular.

Decide what 'E2E encrypted' means and implement it once: either share the room key over the DataChannel after the ready handshake and set encrypted:true on chat/sync, or drop the WASM wire-crypto claim and lean on DTLS + authenticated relay ECDH (pin keys via room secret). Half-implemented crypto is the worst of both.

Add a destroyed flag (or one AbortController governing all timers/listeners) to P2PConnection; check it at the top of createPeerConnection and in every setTimeout callback. Cheap fix for the room-switch leak.
Keep DataChannels alive across signaling WS reconnects: reconnect the WebSocket independently and only re-negotiate peers whose connections actually died.
result.verdict: Refactor connection.ts substantially (or rewrite it around perfect negotiation); keep the signaling server, manager shell, and handler/store wiring. The architecture — signaling for discovery only, full mesh, DataChannels, encrypted relay fallback — is right for this product, and the signaling server plus the offer-collision/ICE-queueing/ready-handshake work shows real competence. But the failure-recovery layer, which is the whole ballgame for 'ultra-reliable', is largely illusory: ICE restart never renegotiates, relay fallback after ICE failure can never fire, any post-handshake failure permanently partitions peers, a signaling blip kills healthy connections, and the one DataChannel silently drops chat messages by construction (maxRetransmits:3). These aren't independent bugs to patch one-by-one — they all stem from removePeer conflating room membership with connection state and from hand-rolled negotiation instead of onnegotiationneeded. A focused rewrite of the connection lifecycle (~1 week: perfect negotiation, two channels, membership/connection separation, destroyed flag, delta sync) would delete several hundred lines, fix five critical issues at once, and create the clean substrate the planned file/media transfer feature needs — the current send path offers nothing reusable for chunked binary transfer anyway. Self-hosting TURN is the one non-code prerequisite; shipping mobile reliability on openrelay free tier is not viable.

result.summary:
The UI layer is a SvelteKit app with three screens: a landing card (src/routes/+page.svelte) offering "Create New Room" (one click: generates UUID via WASM, navigates to /<roomId>) and a join field that accepts full URLs, partial URLs, or bare IDs (extractRoomId, +page.svelte:12-33); a room page (src/routes/[roomId]/+page.svelte) that on mount initializes the user (defaulting to "Anonymous"), creates/joins the room, initializes encryption, loads persisted messages from WASM-backed localStorage, and spins up WebRTC P2P; and a global shell (src/lib/components/layout/AppShell.svelte) that blocks the whole app behind a WASM-loading spinner. The room screen composes a status bar (copy-room-ID button, ConnectionStatus badge with reconnect/sync states, PeerList popover, Leave button with confirmation dialog), a MessageList of MessageBubbles (hover actions on desktop, 400ms long-press popover with haptics on touch), a DraftIndicator strip showing peers' in-progress typing, and a MessageInput textarea that broadcasts content on every keystroke with a 100ms debounce ([roomId]/+page.svelte:182-193).

State is legacy Svelte writable stores (src/lib/stores/) wrapped in factory functions — user and currentRoomId persist to localStorage directly; messages live in a Map<roomId, Message[]> persisted only through WASM's saveRoomMessagesToStorage; drafts is an ephemeral Map with 3s fade-out timers; connection and delivery track P2P state. Components use Svelte 5 runes ($state/$derived/$effect/$props) on top of these stores — a hybrid that mostly works but produced one real reactivity bug (delivery checkmarks read the store via get() inside $derived.by, so they never update; MessageBubble.svelte:82-89).

The biggest structural finding: the PWA layer is entirely dead. manifest.json and the icons/ directory sit at the repo root, not in static/, are never linked from src/app.html, and no build step copies them (verified: zero references in app.html, Dockerfile, nginx.conf, cloud_build.sh, _headers). There is no service worker, no favicon in static/, no apple-touch-icon, no theme-color meta. The elaborate manifest (widgets, share_target, /?action=create shortcuts) references query params no route handles. Similarly, mobile keyboard/viewport handling is incomplete: AppShell uses h-screen (100vh, AppShell.svelte:38) with no dvh or visualViewport handling anywhere in src (verified by grep), and the env(safe-area-inset-*) paddings in MessageInput.svelte:51 and sonner.svelte are no-ops because app.html's viewport meta lacks viewport-fit=cover.

The product's signature twist — live character-by-character typing — is implemented (broadcastTyping → handlers.ts:170-187 → drafts store → DraftIndicator.svelte) but presented as a muted, italic, small-text strip pinned above the input (DraftIndicator.svelte:8-38) that fades to 30% opacity and vanishes after 3 seconds of pause (drafts.ts:16). It is functional but visually buried, and nothing in the room UI tells a first-time user that their own unsent text is being broadcast to peers.
strengths: Frictionless core journey: landing to live room is one click with no signup, and the join field robustly accepts full URLs, scheme-less URLs, or bare IDs (src/routes/+page.svelte:12-33)

Genuine mobile-touch consideration: 44px (h-11) targets across chat chrome, hover actions correctly swapped for a 400ms long-press menu with haptic vibration on touch devices (MessageBubble.svelte:114,147; src/lib/hooks/useLongPress.ts:24-31), and autofocus suppressed on coarse pointers to avoid popping the keyboard (MessageInput.svelte:37-48)
Share flow has a proper fallback chain: Web Share API then clipboard API then execCommand textarea (Header.svelte:23-73)

Rich, honest connection-state communication: reconnect attempts with live countdown, sync progress with message counts, distinct local/offline/failed states, and a manual Reconnect button (ConnectionStatus.svelte:56-138)
Consistent accessibility baseline: sr-only labels on icon buttons, aria-labels on reactions and inputs, motion-reduce: variants on every animation, enterkeyhint="send" on the composer
Clean, complete dark mode via mode-watcher with class-based oklch token pairs (src/app.css:6-62)
Stores are uniformly structured typed factories with immutable updates, and data-testid attributes map to a real Playwright e2e suite (package.json test:e2e:* scripts)

issues.title: PWA setup is entirely dead code: manifest and icons never served or linked
issues.detail: manifest.json and icons/ live at the repo root, not static/, so SvelteKit (adapter-cloudflare) never serves them; src/app.html contains no <link rel="manifest">, no favicon link, no apple-touch-icon, no theme-color meta (verified by grep across src, Dockerfile, nginx.conf, cloud_build.sh, _headers — zero references). There is no service worker. Result: app is not installable, has no offline shell, requests for /favicon.ico 404, and the manifest's shortcuts (/?action=create), share_target, and widgets reference query params no route handles (src/routes/+page.svelte has no URL param handling). For a mobile-first chat product this means no home-screen presence at all.

issues.title: iOS keyboard/viewport handling missing: 100vh shell, no dvh, no visualViewport, dead safe-area insets
issues.detail: AppShell uses h-screen (100vh), which on iOS Safari exceeds the visible viewport when toolbars are shown, pushing the message input off-screen; grep confirms no dvh/svh units, no visualViewport listener, and no interactive-widget meta anywhere in src. Compounding it, app.html's viewport meta lacks viewport-fit=cover, so the env(safe-area-inset-bottom) padding in MessageInput.svelte:51 and the safe-area offset in sonner.svelte always evaluate to 0 — the safe-area code is a no-op. On notched iPhones the composer will sit under the home indicator and behind the open keyboard.

issues.title: Delivery checkmarks are non-reactive — frozen at mount-time value
issues.detail: deliveryStatus is computed in $derived.by via delivery.getDeliveryStatus(message.id), which internally uses svelte/store get() (delivery.ts:55-63). get() does not register a dependency with the runes tracking system, and the only tracked deps are isMe and message.id, which never change. So when delivery-acks arrive (handlers.ts:506 recordDelivery), the bubble's check/double-check icons and the 'Delivered to X/Y peers' tooltip never update — a sent message shows a single gray check forever. The whole delivery-tracking feature is invisible to users.

issues.title: Message list force-scrolls to bottom on every new message, even while reading history
issues.detail: The $effect scrolls to scrollHeight whenever messages.length changes with no isNearBottom check and no 'new messages' affordance. If a user scrolls up to re-read, any incoming message (or their peer's burst of messages) yanks them to the bottom. Standard chat UX requires sticky-bottom only when already at bottom.

issues.title: Message text cannot be selected or copied
issues.detail: The bubble applies select-none unconditionally, so on desktop users cannot select/copy message text at all (there is also no Copy action in MessageActions.svelte — only Edit/Delete). On mobile, long-press opens the action menu instead, and that menu also lacks a Copy option (LongPressMenu.svelte:72-107). Copying received text — a basic chat operation — is impossible everywhere.

issues.title: Stale currentRoomId: leaving via back button or revisiting later leaves Share pointing at an old room
issues.detail: currentRoomId persists to localStorage and is only cleared by the Leave button ([roomId]/+page.svelte:205-209); onDestroy (lines 106-120) does not clear it. Navigating back to '/' (or reopening the app days later on the landing page) leaves $currentRoomId set, so Header.svelte:97 still renders the Share button, which shares a room the user is no longer in. Also [roomId]/+page.svelte does all join logic in onMount keyed off page.params at mount time (line 25), so client-side navigation between two rooms would never re-join/re-init P2P.

issues.title: Two inconsistent invite affordances; the prominent one copies a bare UUID, not a link
issues.detail: The room bar's copy button copies only the raw room ID ('a3f9c2d1...'), while the header's icon-only Share2 button copies the full URL (Header.svelte:27). A new user will naturally tap the visible room pill, send a bare UUID, and force the recipient through the paste-into-join-field flow instead of clicking a link. Inviting is the single most important action in a P2P chat and it is split across two controls with different outputs, neither labeled 'Invite'.

issues.title: The realtime-typing twist is visually buried and undisclosed
issues.detail: Peer drafts render as small italic muted-foreground text in a bg-muted/30 strip above the composer, fading to 30% opacity and deleted after 3s of typing pause (drafts.ts:16 DRAFT_TIMEOUT_MS=3000) — even though the peer still has that text in their input, which contradicts 'radical transparency'. The draft is not in the message stream as a live ghost bubble, gets no animation or color, and nothing in the room UI tells users their own keystrokes are broadcast (the only disclosure is one tagline on the landing card, +page.svelte:88). The product's headline feature reads as a generic typing indicator.

issues.title: No name onboarding; header name input is detached from store state
issues.detail: Users are silently initialized as 'Anonymous' (+page.svelte:41, [roomId]/+page.svelte:34) and the only way to set a name is an unlabeled input in the header that's easy to miss. Header.svelte:9 captures $user.name into $state once at mount, so when initialization later sets 'Anonymous' the input still shows the stale empty value. Peers meanwhile see 'Anonymous' in drafts and messages. There is no prompt at room creation/join, the natural moment to ask.

issues.title: Edit/delete handlers in the route duplicate the persistence layer, including raw localStorage JSON surgery
issues.detail: handleEdit and handleDelete (lines 211-325) each implement: optimistic store update, WASM reload-then-mutate-then-save, and a fallback that parses chatHistory_<roomId> from localStorage and mutates the JSON by hand — duplicating the WASM storage schema in the UI layer twice (lines 239-257 and 295-313). The same user-init block is also copy-pasted three times (+page.svelte:39-45, 60-66; [roomId]/+page.svelte:32-41). This belongs in a single persistence module; the route component is 450 lines of which most is storage plumbing.

issues.title: Composer textarea does not auto-grow for multiline input
issues.detail: textarea has rows=1, resize-none, min-h-11/max-h-32 but no auto-resize logic, so Shift+Enter multiline text scrolls inside a one-line-tall box. Also Enter always sends (handleKeydown line 27), and with enterkeyhint="send" mobile users have no way to insert a newline at all — acceptable as a choice, but combined with no attach button the composer is single-purpose plain text.

issues.title: Tooltips are the sole carrier of status meaning — invisible on touch devices
issues.detail: Connection-state explanations ('You're working offline. Messages are saved locally...'), sync details, error text (lines 165-178), and delivery counts (MessageBubble.svelte:195-216) all live exclusively in bits-ui hover tooltips. On mobile — the platform that most needs the explanation — these are effectively unreachable; tapping a Badge wrapped in Tooltip.Trigger gives nothing. Each bubble also instantiates its own Tooltip.Provider, which should be a single app-level provider.

issues.title: Fonts loaded from Google at runtime in a privacy-positioned app
issues.detail: Inter is fetched from fonts.googleapis.com/fonts.gstatic.com on every load, leaking user IPs to Google from an app marketed as private E2E-encrypted P2P, adding a render-blocking third-party dependency and FOUT. Self-hosting via fontsource is a drop-in fix.

issues.title: Mistyped or dead room IDs silently create a new empty room
issues.detail: wasm.createRoom(roomId) is create-or-join, and any string is accepted as a room ID. A user pasting a truncated invite lands in a fresh empty room showing 'Waiting for peers...' with no way to distinguish a typo from a quiet room. No format validation or 'nobody has ever been here' hint exists.

issues.title: Leave-room confirmation dialog adds friction with misleading copy
issues.detail: Leaving is fully recoverable (messages persist in localStorage; rejoining is pasting the same URL), yet it's gated behind an AlertDialog warning 'Any unsent messages will be lost', which at most means the composer text. Two-step confirm for a reversible action, while genuinely destructive delete already has its own confirm.

issues.title: EmojiPicker roving tabindex never moves DOM focus
issues.detail: Arrow keys update focusedIndex and the visual ring, but no element.focus() is called, so actual keyboard/screen-reader focus does not follow the highlighted option; role="listbox" on a div with button options plus two suppressed a11y warnings (lines 60-61) confirm the pattern is improvised. LongPressMenu similarly uses a hidden Popover.Trigger (LongPressMenu.svelte:68) so focus restoration on close targets a hidden element.

issues.title: No aria-live regions for incoming messages or peer drafts
issues.detail: Neither the message list nor DraftIndicator announces updates to assistive tech; a screen-reader user gets no notification of new messages. Timestamps are also 10px at 70%-opacity muted-foreground (MessageBubble.svelte:184), well below contrast guidelines for the only always-visible metadata.

issues.title: Message content logged to console in production paths
issues.detail: On every room load, the first 30 chars of every stored (decrypted) message are JSON.stringified to console.log ('[Room] Loaded messages from storage'). For an E2E-encrypted app this leaks plaintext into devtools logs and any log-collection tooling; it is explicitly marked Debug but ships unconditionally.

issues.title: Minor dead code and inconsistencies
issues.detail: peerCount is imported but unused; tailwind.config.ts is vestigial under Tailwind v4 (only darkMode/content, no theme); message bubbles get max-h-[400px] overflow-y-auto so very long messages scroll inside the bubble (MessageBubble.svelte:116) — an unusual pattern; manifest theme_color #5E81AC (blue) matches nothing in the all-monochrome oklch palette (app.css:11-62); brand SVG logos exist in icons/ but the header renders plain text 'MINDLINE' (Header.svelte:80).

opportunities: Make the twist first-class: render peer drafts as live ghost bubbles inside the message stream (shimmering border or caret animation), keep them visible while text exists in the peer's composer instead of the 3s fade, and add a one-time in-room hint ('Others can see you type') — this is the product's identity and currently costs ~0 extra architecture since drafts data already flows

Fix the mobile foundation as one small PR: h-dvh instead of h-screen, viewport-fit=cover in app.html, move manifest.json + icons into static/, link manifest + apple-touch-icon + theme-color, add a minimal service worker — together this turns the dead PWA work into a real installable app

Unify invites into a single 'Invite' button that always shares/copies the full URL (drop the bare-ID copy pill), and prompt for display name on first room create/join instead of defaulting to Anonymous

Extract a persistence module (one function each for edit/delete/send that owns the WASM-then-fallback logic) and a shared ensureUserInitialized() — this would cut [roomId]/+page.svelte roughly in half and remove triplicated init code

Migrate shared state from writable-store factories with manual Map cloning to Svelte 5 .svelte.ts rune classes (or at minimum fix get()-based reads inside $derived) — the delivery-checkmark bug is a direct symptom of the hybrid pattern

The planned file/photo/audio features have zero composer affordance today; the single-textarea composer will need an attach button, upload-progress bubble states, and a media bubble type — designing MessageBubble around a message_type switch now (it already has message_type) is cheap preparation
Drop the leave-confirmation dialog and the success toasts for edit/delete/reaction (inline state changes are self-evident); reserve toasts for failures
Self-host Inter via @fontsource and use the existing brand SVG logo in the header to start building a visual identity beyond stock monochrome shadcn
result.verdict: Keep and refactor — no rewrite. The component architecture (shadcn-svelte primitives, store factories, clean separation of chat components) is sound, conventional, and well-tested via Playwright; the issues are concentrated in fixable seams rather than structure. Three focused efforts would transform the product: (1) a mobile/PWA foundation pass (dvh, viewport-fit, serve the manifest/icons, service worker) since the current PWA story is 100% dead code despite an elaborate manifest; (2) promoting the typing-transparency twist from a muted footer strip to ghost bubbles in the message stream, because right now the app's reason to exist looks like a generic typing indicator; (3) consolidating the persistence plumbing out of the 450-line route component and fixing the runes/store hybrid bugs (frozen delivery checkmarks, forced auto-scroll, unselectable text). The visual design is clean but anonymous — stock monochrome shadcn with no brand color or identity — which is acceptable to ship but worth one deliberate design pass before adding media features.

issues.title: Relay fallback sends message/draft content through the server with MITM-able encryption — falsifies the core privacy claim
issues.detail: PRIVACY.md:7 claims "we literally cannot access your messages because they never pass through our servers" and CLAUDE.md says the server "does NOT relay messages". Both are false under the actual code. When direct P2P fails, broadcast() (src/lib/p2p/connection.ts:1164-1184) pushes ALL message types — chat content, char-by-char typing drafts, edits, reactions — through the server's 'relay' handler (signaling-server.js:347-384). The content is plaintext JSON (broadcastChat builds ChatMessage with raw content and never calls WASM encryption — src/lib/p2p/manager.ts:255-263) wrapped only in an AES-GCM envelope whose ECDH P-256 key exchange ('relay-key', connection.ts:128-155, 167-198) is itself relayed by the same server with zero authentication. A malicious or compromised signaling server can substitute public keys and read/modify every relayed message and draft. Worse, the client explicitly accepts UNENCRYPTED relay payloads "for backward compatibility" (connection.ts:560-561), so the server (which controls fromId) can inject arbitrary plaintext messages into any chat. Passive log-only operators can't read content; an active server can. Either drop the relay fallback (rely on TURN, which preserves DTLS E2E) or authenticate the key exchange with a secret the server never sees (e.g., URL-fragment room secret).

issues.title: Signaling WebSocket disconnect tears down all healthy WebRTC connections
issues.detail: ws.onclose calls this.peers.forEach((_, peerId) => this.removePeer(peerId)) — closing every RTCPeerConnection and data channel even though established WebRTC connections do not need the signaling server. A signaling blip (server restart, Fly machine migration, 90-135s keepalive reap during mobile backgrounding) kills active chats and forces full renegotiation through the 7-attempt reconnect loop (manager.ts:19, 415-476), after which the client gives up permanently ("Max reconnection attempts reached"). For a product whose pitch is "ultra-reliable P2P", the server is effectively a hard runtime dependency, contradicting the "discovery only" design. Keep data channels alive across signaling drops and reconnect signaling in the background indefinitely (capped backoff, not capped attempts).

issues.title: Runtime env injection writes to a directory that is never deployed — production signaling URL is silently hardcoded
issues.detail: cloud_build.sh:12-15 builds with adapter-cloudflare (output: .svelte-kit/cloudflare/, verified by CI at ci.yml:51-54) and then runs inject-env.js, which writes window.MINDLINE_ENV to dist/js/env-config.js — a path outside the publish directory. The deployed env-config.js is the empty default from static/js/env-config.js (whose own comment still references the obsolete dist/ layout). Result: SIGNALING_SERVER, USE_SSL and TURN_SERVERS env vars do nothing in production; the app falls through to the hardcoded 'signal-mindline.fly.dev' default in src/lib/p2p/config.ts:126 (and to PUBLIC_TURN_SERVERS with shared openrelayproject credentials, config.ts:11-22). Compounding it, _headers:30-32 marks /*.js as Cache-Control immutable max-age=31536000, so even a fixed env-config.js would be cached by browsers for a year. Replace runtime injection with build-time VITE_ vars or fix the output path + cache headers.

issues.title: No Origin check or auth on WebSocket upgrade; rate-limit IP is client-spoofable; global caps trivially exhaustible
issues.detail: The wss connection handler never inspects req.headers.origin — the CORS allowlist (signaling-server.js:510-516) applies only to the HTTP /health endpoint, not WS upgrades, so any website or script can connect. Rate limiting keys on cf-connecting-ip / x-forwarded-for (lines 199-201), headers an attacker controls when hitting the server directly (Docker deploy exposes it on 9210 with no proxy; the nginx.conf that would front it is orphaned — no nginx service exists in docker-compose.yml). Spoofing a fresh XFF per connection bypasses the 240-conn/min limit entirely, and maxConnections=1000 / maxRooms=500 (lines 23-27) can be exhausted by one host in seconds, denying service and new-room creation (lines 187-191, 272-278) to all users. Minor extras: relay messages are rate-limit-checked twice (lines 241 and 349), halving the effective relay budget to ~25 msg/s — tight for char-by-char typing relayed per-peer; and enforceMapLimit FIFO-evicts live rate-limit entries under churn, resetting counters (lines 63-73). Add an Origin allowlist on upgrade, trust forwarded IPs only behind a known proxy, and cap peers per room.

issues.title: Second, protocol-incompatible signaling server is dead code
issues.detail: signaling-server/server.js is the original prototype (first commit 'chat history'): CommonJS, trusts client-supplied clientId (line 45), routes on data.to/from instead of targetId/fromId (lines 87-95), supports 'message'/'broadcast' types the current client never sends, has CORS '*', no rate limits, no keepalive, no room validation. The current client sends targetId and expects server-assigned IDs via 'client-id'/'yourId', so this server cannot interoperate with it. Nothing references the directory — not package.json scripts, Dockerfile, compose, or CI. It only creates confusion about which server is canonical. Delete the directory.

issues.title: Deployment sprawl: four signaling deploy paths, three of them broken or vestigial, none documented
issues.detail: (1) Docker: Dockerfile installs the ENTIRE frontend prod dependency tree (bits-ui, lucide-svelte, tailwind-merge…) for a server that needs only 'ws', and copies ecosystem.config.js into an image that has no PM2 and runs node directly (CMD line 29). docker-compose.yml mounts ./ssl:ro that the plain-HTTP server never reads. (2) nginx.conf is orphaned: placeholder server_name signal.yourdomain.com, upstream mindline-signaling:3000, but no nginx service in compose — nothing consumes it. (3) PM2 ecosystem.config.js is a third standalone path with duplicated rate-limit env. (4) The apparent real production target, Fly.io (signal-mindline.fly.dev default in config.ts:126), has NO fly.toml or deploy config in the repo; staging deploys run opaque repo-variable commands (STAGING_APP_DEPLOY_CMD / STAGING_SIGNALING_DEPLOY_CMD, staging-e2e.yml:29-30,57-71), and CONTRIBUTING.md:392's deploy step is literally 'Push to production hosting'. The actual production deployment is unreproducible from the repo. Pick one target, commit its config, delete the rest.

issues.title: Static-site-era _redirects and _headers conflict with the worker-based SvelteKit deploy
issues.detail: adapter-cloudflare copies root _headers/_redirects into .svelte-kit/cloudflare (verified in node_modules/@sveltejs/adapter-cloudflare/index.js:136-142 and present in the build output). The SPA catch-all '/* /index.html 200' predates the move to _worker.js routing — no index.html exists in the output (only 404.html), and Cloudflare evaluates _redirects before invoking the worker, so this rule is at best a no-op and at worst breaks deep links to /<roomId>. _headers' blanket '/*' Cache-Control: immutable 1-year and a CSP whitelisting cdn.tailwindcss.com and Google Fonts (neither used since Tailwind moved to the Vite plugin) are similar leftovers. The signaling server also emits a CSP on its JSON health responses (signaling-server.js:507) — harmless, meaningless. Trim these to what the worker deploy actually needs.

issues.title: Room ID is an unauthenticated bearer secret that transits two servers; 'leave' doesn't sever room access
issues.detail: Rooms are any 4-100 char string (signaling-server.js:263); the client uses UUIDs (src/routes/+page.svelte:48) so brute-force enumeration is infeasible and there is no room-listing API — good. But the room UUID is the only access control: anyone who learns it joins silently-equal, and sync-response then hands them full plaintext history over the data channel (src/lib/p2p/handlers.ts:203-210). The UUID is sent in cleartext 'join' messages to the signaling server AND as the URL path /<roomId> to Cloudflare Pages — so two server operators hold every room's bearer token, and the signaling operator could join any room (peers would only see an anonymous 'peer-joined'). Also, the 'leave' handler (signaling-server.js:386-405) removes the socket from the room but never clears the server-side currentRoom binding, so a departed client can keep relaying messages and signaling into the room until it disconnects. Moving the secret to a URL fragment (never sent to servers) and keying rooms on its hash fixes the first; one-line fix for the second.

issues.title: Stale CORS allowlist and config drift on the signaling server
issues.detail: The HTTP allowlist contains mindline.pages.dev plus localhost:8080/8088 — but dev runs on 5173 and preview on 4173, and the custom domain implied by src/routes/+page.svelte:16 (mindline.chat) is absent. Moot for WebSockets (CORS doesn't gate WS upgrades) but it documents that this list was never maintained, and it would block any future fetch-based endpoint from the real origins.

opportunities: Delete the vestigial deployment surface in one sweep: signaling-server/ directory, nginx.conf, ecosystem.config.js (and its COPY in the Dockerfile), the ./ssl compose mount, and the _redirects SPA fallback. None are load-bearing; together they are most of the 'deployment story' confusion.

Drop the WebSocket relay fallback entirely and rely on TURN for NAT-blocked paths. TURN preserves true E2E (DTLS keys never touch the relay), it deletes ~250 lines of hand-rolled ECDH/AES relay crypto on the client plus the server relay path, removes the MITM hole and the plaintext backward-compat acceptance, and restores the headline privacy claim to literal truth. The 16KB maxPayload makes relay useless for the planned file/photo/audio/video features anyway — those must go over data channels with TURN.
Give the signaling server its own 5-line package.json (only 'ws') and build the Docker image from it — the current image installs the whole frontend UI dependency tree for a 600-line server.

Move the room secret to the URL fragment (#secret), derive the room encryption key from it client-side, and join the signaling room by its hash. Neither Cloudflare nor the signaling server ever sees the bearer token; the server-can-join-any-room and sync-hands-history-to-any-joiner problems disappear without any server change.

Keep WebRTC connections alive across signaling drops: remove the peers.forEach(removePeer) from ws.onclose and reconnect signaling in the background with uncapped, jittered backoff. This single change converts most signaling-server downtime into a non-event for active chats.

Consider consolidating onto Cloudflare end-to-end: the signaling server is a natural Durable Object (WebSocket hibernation makes it near-free at this scale), which collapses Fly+Docker+PM2+nginx into the same `wrangler deploy` that ships the app, gives a committed-to-repo deploy config, real client IPs, and automatic TLS.

Replace runtime env injection (inject-env.js + window.MINDLINE_ENV + static/js/env-config.js) with build-time VITE_SIGNALING_SERVER / VITE_TURN_SERVERS. The runtime path is currently broken anyway, and build-time vars remove the uncacheable-config problem and ~100 lines of plumbing.

Add the two cheap server hardenings that matter: Origin allowlist on the WS upgrade and a per-room peer cap (mesh topology degrades badly past ~6-8 peers regardless), plus only trust cf-connecting-ip/x-forwarded-for when behind the proxy that sets them.
strengths: The canonical signaling-server.js is genuinely well-hardened for its size: server-assigned UUID client IDs make peer-ID spoofing impossible (lines 195-223, fromId always overwritten at 336-341, 365), 16KB payload cap, guarded JSON parsing, three-tier env-tunable rate limiting with bounded maps and periodic cleanup, empty-room GC, mobile-tolerant ping keepalive (45s interval, 2 missed pongs), graceful SIGTERM, and a deliberately information-minimal /health endpoint.

The testing rigor around signaling is far above average for a side project: a parameterized soak test with pass/fail thresholds (scripts/signaling-soak-test.mjs), a careful e2e wrapper that detects stale port-3000 processes and refuses localhost URLs in remote mode (scripts/test-e2e-with-signaling.sh), blocking required-E2E suites in CI (ci.yml:44-45), and a scheduled staging workflow that runs the soak test and a 3-browser matrix against deployed infrastructure (staging-e2e.yml).

Client-side connection management shows real-world mobile scar tissue done right: exponential-backoff reconnect with store-surfaced state (manager.ts:415-476), visibility-change reconnection only when the socket is actually dead (manager.ts:520+), deterministic higher-ID-initiates rule with collision rollback to prevent offer glare, and ICE restart with capped exponential backoff (connection.ts:736-780).

Relay payloads, when the fallback engages, are at least wrapped in per-peer AES-GCM (connection.ts:224-274) rather than sent bare — the intent and mechanism are right even though the key exchange is unauthenticated.
result.summary:
Mindline's signaling layer is one ~600-line Node/ws server, signaling-server.js, run via `pnpm run signaling` (package.json:17), the Dockerfile, ecosystem.config.js, and the e2e wrapper script. It is the canonical server: it speaks the exact protocol the client implements (server-assigned UUID client IDs delivered via 'client-id'/'yourId', targeted routing via targetId/fromId, 'relay-key' and 'relay' message types matching src/lib/p2p/connection.ts and types.ts). It does peer discovery (join/peer-joined/room-joined/peer-left), relays WebRTC offers/answers/ICE between specific peers, and — importantly — also relays application messages via a 'relay' fallback when direct P2P fails. The second server, signaling-server/server.js, is the original prototype from the first commit: CommonJS, client-supplied IDs, routes on data.to/from rather than targetId/fromId, no rate limiting, CORS '*'. It is referenced by nothing in the repo and is protocol-incompatible with the current client; it exists purely because nobody deleted it.

On the privacy claim: in the happy path (direct WebRTC) no content touches the server — only room IDs, IPs, and SDP/ICE metadata. But the relay fallback is on by default (allowRelayFallback, types.ts:34), and broadcast() pushes every message type — chat content, char-by-char typing drafts, edits, reactions — through the server whenever a peer lacks a data channel (connection.ts:1164-1184). That content is plaintext JSON (broadcastChat never invokes the WASM crypto; manager.ts:255-263) inside an AES-GCM envelope whose ECDH keys are exchanged through the very server being distrusted, unauthenticated (connection.ts:128-155), and the client still accepts plaintext relay payloads for 'backward compatibility' (connection.ts:560-561). So PRIVACY.md's 'messages never pass through our servers' is false whenever P2P fails, and the encryption that does apply is MITM-able by the server operator. Additionally the room UUID — the only access credential — is sent in cleartext to the signaling server and as the URL path to Cloudflare Pages.

Security posture of the canonical server is otherwise decent (server-assigned IDs kill spoofing, 16KB payload cap, three-tier rate limiting, bounded maps, info-minimal /health) but has structural gaps: no Origin check on the WS upgrade (CORS at lines 510-516 covers only HTTP), rate-limit IPs read from spoofable cf-connecting-ip/x-forwarded-for headers (199-201) while the Docker deploy exposes the server directly, global caps (1000 conns/500 rooms) one attacker can exhaust, no per-room peer cap, and 'leave' that never clears the server-side room binding. Room enumeration is impractical (client uses UUIDs, no list API) but rooms are unauthenticated bearer-token spaces. Heartbeating is server-driven ping/pong (45s, 2 misses, mobile-tolerant); client reconnect is exponential backoff capped at 7 attempts then permanent give-up, with a visibility handler for mobile foregrounding — but a signaling WS drop tears down all healthy WebRTC connections (connection.ts:380-392), making the 'discovery-only' server a de facto runtime dependency.

Deployment is the weakest area: the frontend genuinely ships to Cloudflare Pages via adapter-cloudflare (CI verifies .svelte-kit/cloudflare/_worker.js) with cloud_build.sh as the Pages build command; the signaling server apparently runs on Fly.io (hardcoded signal-mindline.fly.dev fallback in config.ts:126) yet no fly.toml exists in the repo. Around that real path orbit four vestigial or broken ones: a Docker/compose setup that installs the entire frontend dependency tree and mounts unused SSL certs, an orphaned nginx.conf with placeholder domains that no compose service consumes, a PM2 ecosystem.config.js copied into a Docker image that runs plain node, and a runtime env-injection script that writes to dist/ — a directory the Cloudflare build never publishes — so production signaling/TURN configuration silently falls back to hardcoded defaults. Staging deploys run opaque commands stored in GitHub repo variables; CONTRIBUTING.md's deploy instructions are 'Push to production hosting'."
result.verdict: Keep the canonical signaling-server.js as the basis — it is small, the hardening work (server-assigned IDs, rate limits, keepalive, soak/CI coverage) is real and worth preserving — but refactor aggressively around it rather than rewriting from scratch. Three decisions matter far more than any code polish: (1) drop the WebSocket relay fallback (or authenticate its key exchange with a never-sent-to-server secret), because as built it makes the product's headline privacy guarantee untrue and its crypto MITM-able by the operator, and its 16KB cap is useless for the planned media features anyway — TURN already covers the NAT-blocked case while preserving E2E; (2) stop tearing down healthy WebRTC connections when the signaling socket drops and reconnect signaling in the background indefinitely, which converts most server downtime into a non-event; (3) collapse the deployment story to one committed-to-repo target (Fly with a fly.toml, or better, a Cloudflare Durable Object so app and signaling share a single wrangler deploy) and delete the prototype server, nginx.conf, PM2 config, the broken inject-env path, and the static-era _redirects. The minimal design this product needs is a ~150-line discovery-only WS service — join/offer/answer/ice/leave, server-assigned IDs, Origin allowlist, per-IP token bucket behind a trusted proxy, ~8-peer room cap, ping keepalive — plus TURN; everything beyond that in the current tree is either vestigial or actively undermining the privacy and reliability goals."

result.summary:
The JS↔WASM bridge is three files: src/lib/wasm/index.ts (363 lines: loader + "safe proxy"), src/lib/wasm/crypto.ts (WebCrypto functions attached to window for Rust to call back into), and src/lib/wasm/types.ts (a hand-written WasmModule interface). At app start, AppShell.svelte (mounted from +layout.svelte, so it wraps every route) awaits loadWasm(), which dynamically imports pkg/mindline.js and fetches the 1.05 MB mindline_bg.wasm. All UI is gated behind this load. Access then goes through the `wasm` proxy object, where each method calls createSafeCall(funcName, paramNames)(args): a per-invocation closure that checks arity, optionally coerces params, looks the function up by string on the module cast to Record<string, unknown>, and re-wraps errors.

Export catalog vs. reality: pkg/mindline.d.ts declares 74 function exports. The proxy wraps 51 of them. Grep of all of src/ shows exactly 15 are ever called at runtime: initialize, set_message_manager_user, generate_uuid, create_room_with_id, initialize_room_encryption, load/save_room_messages_to_storage, get_room_messages, send_message_enhanced, receive_message_from_peer, edit_message, delete_message, add_reaction, remove_reaction, decrypt_message_content. Of those, create_room_with_id is a literal no-op in Rust (core.rs:245-253, it only console_logs) and decrypt_message_content sits on a dead path (the `encrypted` flag on P2P messages is never set to true anywhere in src/). The other 36 wrapped methods (drafts, peers, validation, sync, logging, key management, encrypt_message_content) plus ~23 unwrapped exports (performance monitoring, log search/export, initialize_storage — whose Rust backend, storage.rs:42-49, is an in-memory fake) have zero call sites. Whole Rust subsystems ship dead: logger.rs (701 lines), performance.rs (343), sanitizer.rs (283, reachable only via the never-called validate_* exports — so message input is never sanitized on the real path), plus deprecated legacy send_message/get_messages/join_room (core.rs:91-241).

What the 13 genuinely load-bearing exports do: store {id, name} into two redundant Rust globals (core.rs:34-64), generate a UUID, maintain an in-memory message map mirroring the Svelte messages store, and JSON-serialize it to localStorage under chatHistory_{roomId} (messages.rs:524-580) — in plaintext. The headline feature the Rust layer exists for, AES-256-GCM encryption, is a façade: initialize_room_encryption generates and persists a per-room key ([roomId]/+page.svelte:50), but encrypt_message_content has zero call sites, broadcastChat sends plaintext with no encrypted flag (manager.ts:255-263), sync responses are explicitly sent encrypted:false (handlers.ts:203-211, comment admits "key sharing not yet implemented"), and storage is plaintext. Worse, when Rust does encrypt/decrypt it calls back into JS WebCrypto via window globals (crypto.ts:98-109, pkg/mindline.js:1566-1576) — the round-trip is JS→WASM→JS, the Rust layer adds nothing cryptographic itself. Every message mutation is performed twice (Svelte store + WASM mirror) with defensive loadRoomMessagesFromStorage-before-edit calls and duplicated hand-rolled localStorage fallback writers in the page ([roomId]/+page.svelte:227-258, 283-314) — clear evidence the authors don't trust their own bridge.

Failure modes: loadWasm has no timeout, no retry, and no progress UI. On slow networks the user stares at a generic "Loading..." spinner while ~1.1 MB downloads and compiles before even the home page's name field renders. On any load failure the entire app bricks with "Failed to load application" plus the raw error.message and a manual reload link (AppShell.svelte:49-60) — even though chat transport, typing indicators, and all stores are pure TypeScript and could run degraded. Post-load, isWasmReady() guards make failures silent: home-page buttons no-op (+page.svelte:36,57) and handlers silently skip persistence (handlers.ts:137,285,330...), losing messages on refresh without telling the user. For old Safari specifically: build target is esnext (vite.config.ts:11) and the wasm-bindgen glue has a FinalizationRegistry fallback (pkg/mindline.js:110-112); WebAssembly support (Safari 11+) predates the WebRTC features the app needs anyway, so the binding constraint is payload size and the all-or-nothing gate, not API support.
strengths: Single choke point: every WASM touch goes through src/lib/wasm/index.ts, so the layer is trivially replaceable — no scattered direct imports of pkg/mindline.js anywhere else in src/.

AppShell gating (+layout.svelte → AppShell.svelte:42-64) means pages only mount after the load settles, eliminating most wasm-not-ready races by construction; loadWasm is also correctly browser-only and idempotent (index.ts:28-37).

Error wrapping in createSafeCall (index.ts:123-128) at least prefixes failures with the function name, and every call site wraps proxy calls in try/catch with a UI-visible fallback (toasts, status='Failed'), so WASM exceptions never take down the page.
crypto.ts is clean, correct WebCrypto usage (AES-256-GCM with 128-bit tags, PBKDF2 600k iterations, getRandomValues) — directly reusable as the foundation of a real TS-side E2E implementation.
The dynamic import keeps the 1 MB binary out of the SSR path and out of the main bundle.

issues.title: E2E encryption is a façade across the bridge: keys are generated but no message is ever encrypted
issues.detail: initialize_room_encryption is called on room join ([roomId]/+page.svelte:50) and persists an AES key to localStorage (crypto_api.rs:43-71), but encryptMessageContent (index.ts:294-298) has zero call sites. broadcastChat sends plaintext with no `encrypted` flag (manager.ts:255-263); sync responses are explicitly encrypted:false (handlers.ts:205-211); WASM persistence writes plaintext JSON to localStorage (messages.rs:524-580). Verified by grep: 'encrypted: true' appears nowhere in src/.
