The product's core security claim (CLAUDE.md: 'Messages encrypted with AES-256-GCM, per-room keys') is not true on the wire or at rest; only WebRTC's transport DTLS applies. The key also never leaves the device (no key exchange), so peer decryption could never work.

issues.title: decryptMessageContent returns a Promise but is consumed synchronously — guaranteed corruption the day encryption is switched on
issues.detail: Rust decrypt_message_content returns js_sys::Promise (crypto_api.rs:97-111; pkg/mindline.d.ts:11 'Promise<any>'). The proxy types it as string (index.ts:291-292) and handlers.ts:100-103 and :253-257 do `const result = wasm.decryptMessageContent(content); if (result) decryptedContent = result;` — a Promise is always truthy, so message content would become '[object Promise]'. Currently unreachable (see critical issue), but it is a landmine sitting directly on the path of the planned fix, and it proves the proxy's hand-written types actively mask bridge bugs instead of catching them.

issues.title: The 'safe proxy' erases type safety instead of adding it; three drifting sources of truth for one API
issues.detail: createSafeCall dispatches by string on the module cast to Record<string, unknown> (index.ts:118-124), so wasm-bindgen's generated types (pkg/mindline.d.ts) are completely bypassed. Verified drift: validate_* actually return string (pkg/mindline.d.ts:78-80) but the proxy declares boolean (index.ts:209-216); getRoomMessages declared string (index.ts:234-237) but returns a JS array — the consumer carries a workaround comment and Array.isArray check ([roomId]/+page.svelte:59-60); decryptMessageContent declared string vs actual Promise. types.ts' WasmModule interface is a third, also-stale copy: it lists never-wrapped functions (record_performance_metric, search_logs) and omits functions the proxy does wrap (edit_message, add_reaction, all key-management). The proxy's actual runtime value is two checks: 'module loaded' and 'function exists'.

issues.title: WASM load is a hard gate that bricks the whole app: no timeout, no retry, ~1.1 MB before first paint of any page
issues.detail: AppShell (wrapping every route via +layout.svelte:21) shows a spinner until loadWasm resolves — fetching+compiling mindline_bg.wasm (1,080,091 bytes) plus 57 KB glue — and on failure renders 'Failed to load application' with the raw error.message (AppShell.svelte:49-60). loadWasm (index.ts:28-60) has no timeout, no retry, no progress reporting; recovery is a manual full-page reload. Yet the features users see — WebRTC chat, character-by-character typing, presence, toasts — are pure TypeScript and would work fine without WASM. On slow mobile networks this is tens of seconds of dead spinner for functionality (uuid + localStorage JSON) JS does natively.

issues.title: ~80% of the bridge is dead weight: 36 of 51 proxy methods and ~59 of 74 WASM exports have zero call sites
issues.detail: Grep across src/ finds 15 proxy methods called; unused wrappers include the entire logging API (index.ts:322-354), validation (209-216), drafts/peers state mirrors (177-198), sync request/response (242-252), and all key management except initializeRoomEncryption (304-319). Unwrapped-and-unused exports back whole dead Rust subsystems shipped in the binary: logger.rs (701 lines), performance.rs (343), sanitizer.rs (283 — meaning message sanitization never actually runs), storage.rs's IndexedDBManager which is an in-memory fake per its own comment (storage.rs:42-43), and deprecated legacy send_message/get_messages/join_room (core.rs:91-241). create_room_with_id, called by the UI in two places (+page.svelte:49, [roomId]/+page.svelte:45), is a no-op that only logs (core.rs:245-253).

issues.title: Dual-state write-twice pattern: every mutation hits the Svelte store AND the WASM mirror, with hand-rolled fallbacks proving distrust
issues.detail: Every edit/delete/reaction/receive is applied to the messages store, then re-applied to WASM state, then saveRoomMessagesToStorage (9 call sites). Edits/deletes first call loadRoomMessagesFromStorage to resync WASM before mutating ([roomId]/+page.svelte:230, 287) — a band-aid for the two stores diverging — and both handlers contain duplicated ~20-line manual localStorage fallback writers for when the WASM path throws ([roomId]/+page.svelte:239-257, 295-313). Rust itself triplicates identity state (CHAT_MANAGER + APP_STATE in core.rs:34-64, plus MessageManager via set_message_manager_user). One owner for message state would delete all of this.

issues.title: isWasmReady() guards fail silently, hiding data loss
issues.detail: Incoming chat/edit/delete/reaction handlers skip WASM persistence when not ready (handlers.ts:137, 285, 305, 330, 391, 429, 483) — the message renders but vanishes on refresh, with no user-facing signal. Home page buttons silently no-op (+page.svelte:36, 57) and the room page silently renders a dead chat UI ([roomId]/+page.svelte:26-29). The AppShell gate makes these mostly unreachable today, but they are landmines for any refactor that relaxes the gate.

issues.title: createSafeCall machinery is per-call overhead with near-zero payoff
issues.detail: Every proxy method constructs a fresh closure per invocation (e.g. index.ts:137-141) rather than once. The arity check (76-80) can never fail because each wrapper has a fixed TypeScript signature. The 40-line param-transform system (83-111) is used for exactly one real transform (limit: Number, index.ts:236) and two identity functions (230-232, 250-252).

issues.title: Dead crypto bridge function and window-global pollution
issues.detail: webcryptoDeriveKey is installed on window (crypto.ts:104-105) but the compiled WASM never imports it (grep of pkg/mindline.js: 0 hits; only webcryptoEncrypt/Decrypt/RandomBytes at pkg/mindline.js:1566-1576). The bridge also depends on mutable window globals being set before init — a fragile implicit contract (setupCryptoBridge must run before wasm.default(), index.ts:41-45).

issues.title: vite.config excludes a nonexistent package '@anthropic-ai/claude-wasm'
issues.detail: optimizeDeps.exclude lists '@anthropic-ai/claude-wasm', which is not in package.json dependencies — leftover generated cruft that should be removed to avoid confusing future readers about how the WASM is loaded.

issues.title: get_room_messages contains a sentinel-value hack guarding against the bridge's own Option encoding
issues.detail: Rust treats any limit > 1,000,000 as None because 'the JS binding uses 0x100000001 as a sentinel' (message_api.rs:54-65). The JS proxy always passes a concrete limit ([roomId]/+page.svelte:58 passes 100), so this is defensive scar tissue from past bridge bugs — emblematic of the marshaling tax paid at every boundary crossing.

opportunities: Delete the WASM layer entirely. The 13 load-bearing exports reduce to: crypto.randomUUID(), a Map<roomId, Message[]> with CRUD + reaction merging, and JSON.stringify to localStorage — ~150 lines of TypeScript. This removes the 1.05 MB download, the all-or-nothing boot gate, the dual-state write-twice pattern, the Rust toolchain from CI (verify:web currently rebuilds wasm on every run), and the marshaling boundary that the planned file/photo/audio features would otherwise have to shovel binary blobs across for zero benefit.

Implement real E2E encryption in TypeScript using the already-written, correct crypto.ts WebCrypto helpers — which is what the Rust 'crypto' does anyway via window callbacks. Encrypt in broadcastChat, await decryption in handlers (fixing the Promise bug by construction), and add key exchange (e.g. embed the key in the room URL fragment so it never hits the server) — without key sharing, per-room keys are unusable across peers.

If keeping WASM for future reasons: re-export the wasm-bindgen functions directly (pkg/mindline.d.ts already provides accurate types) instead of the string-dispatch proxy; delete types.ts' parallel interface; trim the Rust crate to the used exports (drop logger.rs, performance.rs, sanitizer.rs, storage.rs, legacy core.rs paths) to shrink the binary several-fold; and lazy-load after first paint instead of gating the home page.

Make WASM (or its TS replacement) failure-degradable: boot the UI immediately, load persistence/crypto in the background, queue writes until ready, and surface a non-blocking banner on failure instead of bricking the app with a raw error message.

Pick one owner for message state. Today three copies exist (Svelte store, WASM MessageManager, localStorage) reconciled by load-before-write calls and duplicated fallback writers; a single store with a persistence subscription eliminates the whole class of divergence bugs.
result.verdict: Rewrite — specifically, remove. This bridge is ~90% ceremony by every measure: 59 of 74 WASM exports and 36 of 51 proxy wrappers are never called; one of the 15 live calls is a Rust no-op and another sits on a dead code path. What remains load-bearing is UUID generation and plaintext JSON persistence to localStorage — commodity JS — purchased at the cost of a 1.05 MB binary that hard-gates first paint of every page, a hand-written proxy that erases the generated types and has already accumulated three verified type lies (including a Promise-as-string bug that will corrupt messages the moment encryption is actually enabled), and a write-twice dual-state pattern the authors themselves hedge against with duplicated localStorage fallbacks. The one justification for Rust here — encryption — is both unused on every real message path and delegated back to JS WebCrypto via window globals when it does run. Porting the live surface to a ~150-line TypeScript module plus using crypto.ts directly for real E2E would make the app faster to load, more reliable on mobile, simpler to maintain, and structurally ready for the planned P2P file/media features; keeping the WASM layer preserves none of those properties and all of the risk.

result.summary:
Mindline's test infrastructure is a Playwright-only E2E suite (no TS unit tests anywhere — no vitest/jest in package.json; the only unit tests are 19 Rust tests, all in /Users/jakob/Code/lawzava/mindline/src-rust/messages.rs:643+, covering message struct logic). The E2E layer has 14 spec files in /Users/jakob/Code/lawzava/mindline/tests/e2e/ split into a blocking "required" suite (connection-status, landing-page, messaging, room-page, required-persistence, required-p2p, required-network — wired via E2E_STRICT_P2P=1 in package.json:20) and a non-blocking "best-effort" suite (typing-indicators, p2p-multiuser, peer-list, reactions, edit-delete, mobile-p2p) that runs with continue-on-error in CI (.github/workflows/ci.yml:47-49). Orchestration is via scripts/test-e2e-with-signaling.sh, which starts a real local signaling server, health-gates it, and supports a remote/staging mode (SKIP_SIGNALING_START=1) used by a daily staging workflow with a Desktop Chrome + Mobile Safari + Mobile Chrome matrix (.github/workflows/staging-e2e.yml:76-118). There is also a WebSocket soak test with pass/fail thresholds (scripts/signaling-soak-test.mjs).

The P2P tests are genuinely end-to-end: two or three real browser contexts connect through the real signaling server and establish real RTCPeerConnections on localhost — nothing in the data path is mocked. The one deliberate mock is excellent: required-network.spec.ts breaks RTCPeerConnection and shims WebSocket.prototype.send to capture relay frames, then asserts the relay carries AES-GCM ciphertext and never plaintext (tests/e2e/required-network.spec.ts:19-101) — the only place the E2E suite verifies encryption on the wire. However, every room join goes through joinRoom()/createRoom() which appends ?fastConnect=true (tests/e2e/helpers/test-utils.ts:90,120), activating a test-only timing config baked into production code (src/lib/p2p/config.ts:183-198, src/lib/p2p/connection.ts:436-438,677), so production connection timing/backoff is essentially never exercised.

Coverage in the blocking path is narrow: 2-peer message exchange, rejoin history sync (required-p2p.spec.ts), relay fallback with encryption, offline/online survival (required-network.spec.ts), solo messaging UI, persistence of name/messages/keys, and landing/room navigation. The product's headline feature — character-by-character typing transparency — lives only in the best-effort suite, where every test self-skips if peers fail to connect (e.g. tests/e2e/typing-indicators.spec.ts:39-44), and even there no test verifies progressive updates: one test fills the input in a single event and checks final content (typing-indicators.spec.ts:47,57), the other types character-by-character but only asserts the indicator is visible (typing-indicators.spec.ts:80-87). Mobile coverage is largely cosmetic: the interaction specs force maxTouchPoints=0 to defeat the app's touch detection (reactions.spec.ts:11-18 and 4 other specs), so touch-native flows are never tested, and mobile-p2p.spec.ts mostly asserts console-log strings or tautologies (mobile-p2p.spec.ts:184-193, 214). PR CI runs Desktop Chrome only (package.json:26).
strengths: Real P2P, not mocked: required-p2p and multiuser tests use multiple real browser contexts, a real signaling server, and real WebRTC DataChannels end to end (tests/e2e/required-p2p.spec.ts:17-44, tests/e2e/helpers/test-utils.ts:137-159).

The relay fallback test is the best test in the repo: it disables RTCPeerConnection, intercepts WebSocket.send, and asserts relay frames contain AES-GCM ciphertext and never the plaintext message — directly verifying the 'server never sees content' security property (tests/e2e/required-network.spec.ts:19-101). This interception harness is reusable for file-transfer tests.

Required vs best-effort architecture with E2E_STRICT_P2P is a sensible design: handleUnavailableP2P fails hard in strict mode and skips otherwise (tests/e2e/helpers/test-utils.ts:269-275), and the required suite genuinely blocks CI (.github/workflows/ci.yml:44-45).

Solid orchestration script: stale-port guard, health-check gating with timeout, remote-mode validation that refuses localhost URLs unless explicitly overridden, clean signal-trap teardown (scripts/test-e2e-with-signaling.sh:32-99).

Signaling soak test with quantitative pass/fail thresholds (join rate, received/sent ratio, error rates) runnable locally and against staging (scripts/signaling-soak-test.mjs:156-184, staging-e2e.yml:120-162).
Daily scheduled staging E2E with a 3-browser matrix (Desktop Chrome, Mobile Safari, Mobile Chrome) against the deployed environment — real network, real TLS/WSS (staging-e2e.yml:19-20,76-118).
Consistent data-testid locator discipline and a shared helper module rather than copy-pasted selectors (tests/e2e/helpers/test-utils.ts).

issues.title: Headline feature (realtime typing sync) has zero blocking coverage and no progressive-update assertion
issues.detail: All typing-indicator tests are in the best-effort suite (package.json:21), which runs with continue-on-error in CI (ci.yml:47-49) and self-skips whenever peers fail to connect (typing-indicators.spec.ts:39-44). Worse, no test verifies the character-by-character behavior the product is named for: 'should show typing indicator when peer types' uses fill() — a single input event — then checks final content (line 47,57); 'should show typing content in real-time' uses pressSequentially but asserts only that the indicator is visible, never that intermediate content streamed (lines 80-87). A regression that batches the entire draft into one final update, or breaks typing sync entirely, would not fail any blocking test.

issues.title: Best-effort suite can pass green while executing zero P2P assertions
issues.detail: Every best-effort P2P test follows the pattern 'if (!isConnected) { cleanup; test.skip(); return; }' (p2p-multiuser.spec.ts:40-45, 81-86, peer-list.spec.ts:90-95, typing-indicators.spec.ts:39-44, etc.). If the signaling server or WebRTC silently breaks in the test environment, the entire suite skips and reports success. Combined with continue-on-error in CI, edit/delete sync, reaction sync, 3-peer broadcast, peer list, and disconnect handling have effectively zero enforcement. The skip exists to paper over real flakiness: P2P connection establishment is unreliable enough that the authors couldn't make these tests required.

issues.title: All P2P tests run with test-only ?fastConnect=true timing; production connection path untested
issues.detail: joinRoom() and createRoom() always append ?fastConnect=true (test-utils.ts:90,120), which switches the app to test timing: 100ms vs 2000ms base offer delays (src/lib/p2p/connection.ts:436-438), 500ms vs 2000ms retry delay (connection.ts:677), shorter offerTimeout and meshCheckInterval (src/lib/p2p/config.ts:183-189). The real-user connection sequence — the timing under which races, glare, and retries actually occur — is never exercised by any test, including the 'required' suite. It also means a test-mode backdoor is reachable in production via URL param (config.ts:192-198).

issues.title: Mobile projects are neutered: tests force desktop touch detection, so no touch interaction is ever tested
issues.detail: Five specs (reactions, edit-delete, persistence, typing-indicators, p2p-multiuser) inject Object.defineProperty(navigator, 'maxTouchPoints', {value: 0}) and delete ontouchstart in beforeEach, explicitly defeating the app's touch detection even when running under the Mobile Safari / Mobile Chrome projects (playwright.config.ts:25-31). Tests that still detect touch self-skip via isActionsAvailable() (edit-delete.spec.ts:22-35). Net result: how a phone user reacts to, edits, or deletes a message is completely untested — and PR CI only runs Desktop Chrome anyway (package.json:26, ci.yml:45). For a product that must 'work great on mobile', the only real mobile signal is the daily staging run of the required suite, which contains no reactions/edit/typing tests.

issues.title: Encryption is only verified on the relay fallback path, never on the primary WebRTC path; crypto.rs has zero unit tests
issues.detail: The AES-256-GCM implementation (src-rust/crypto.rs) has no #[test] functions — all 19 Rust tests are in messages.rs. E2E-side, ciphertext is asserted only for WebSocket relay frames (required-network.spec.ts:88-101); no test inspects DataChannel payloads, so a regression sending plaintext over the primary P2P path would pass everything. Encryption 'tests' in persistence.spec.ts only check that a localStorage key exists and messages re-render after reload (persistence.spec.ts:229-264) — they would pass identically if encryption were a no-op. TESTING.md section 13.3's 'Room A key doesn't decrypt B' cross-room isolation test is documented but not implemented (only key-difference is checked, persistence.spec.ts:286-308).

issues.title: Pervasive sleep-based synchronization and a 2.2s navigation throttle papering over a rate-limit conflict
issues.detail: waitForP2PSync() is a bare setTimeout sleep (test-utils.ts:207-209) used ~30 times with magic values (500ms-5000ms) instead of condition polling — both slow and flaky under load. throttleRoomNavigation() inserts a mandatory 2.2s delay before every room join because the production signaling server's per-IP connection rate limit throttles the test suite itself (test-utils.ts:11-24) — the tests are fighting production anti-abuse config rather than the server exposing a test-friendly knob, adding ~2.2s × every join × 3 browser projects to wall-clock time. setupTwoUsers adds another fixed 3s sleep (test-utils.ts:156). Combined with workers:1, fullyParallel:false and retries:1 (playwright.config.ts:10-12), the suite is slow and one full retry hides residual flake.

issues.title: mobile-p2p.spec.ts tests assert console-log strings and tautologies, not behavior
issues.detail: 'should cleanup on page navigation' passes if ANY of six log strings appears — including '[P2P Manager] Page lifecycle handlers registered', which logs at setup, not cleanup, so the test passes even if cleanup never runs (lines 184-193). 'mobile device detection returns correct config' asserts typeof isMobile === 'boolean' — it re-implements the regex in the test and can never fail (lines 196-215). The visibility-change test only asserts the status badge is still visible after synthetic events ('page didn't crash', lines 89-92). Real mobile lifecycle behavior (reconnect after backgrounding, draft preservation) is untested.

issues.title: Several tests are structured so they cannot fail
issues.detail: 'should show correct count with multiple peers' checks if text includes '2 peers' and otherwise just console.logs and passes (peer-list.spec.ts:204-210). required-network's offline-recovery test accepts Connected|Reconnecting|Connecting|Local as the final state (required-network.spec.ts:137-143) — a client stuck in 'Reconnecting' forever passes; no message is sent after recovery to prove the session actually works. mobile-p2p's offline test has the same accept-anything pattern (mobile-p2p.spec.ts:155-162). p2p-multiuser's 'should not duplicate messages on resync' (line 100) never triggers a resync — it just sends one more message and checks count+1.

issues.title: TESTING.md materially overstates coverage
issues.detail: Claims '~105 tests implemented' with every file marked ✅, but large documented areas have no corresponding spec code: reconnect button behavior (section 4.4), Disconnected/Failed badge states via killing the signaling server (4.1), error icon/tooltip (4.5), join/leave toasts (10.1), typing debounce network-traffic checks (16), cross-room key isolation (13.3), theme system-preference (2.3), Web Share API (2.2), mesh monitor retry limits (15.4), screen reader/keyboard nav (18.2-18.3). Anyone trusting this doc would believe reconnection UX is tested; no test ever stops the signaling server.

issues.title: No Firefox or desktop WebKit coverage; PR CI is Chromium-only
issues.detail: Projects are Desktop Chrome, iPhone 13 (WebKit mobile emulation), Pixel 5 (Chromium mobile emulation). Firefox — which has meaningfully different WebRTC/DataChannel behavior — is absent entirely. PR CI runs only Desktop Chrome (package.json:26); WebKit gets exercised only in the daily staging run. Both CDP-based offline tests skip on non-Chromium (required-network.spec.ts:111, mobile-p2p.spec.ts:101), so network-loss recovery has zero coverage on the browsers mobile users actually run.

issues.title: Duplicated offline/online and connection-status helpers across specs
issues.detail: mobile-p2p.spec.ts:95-163 and required-network.spec.ts:106-144 are near-identical CDP offline/online tests; mobile-p2p also re-implements waitForConnectionStatus locally (lines 9-20) instead of importing the helper. The WebSocket-shim init script is duplicated verbatim for page and contextB in required-network.spec.ts:19-68. Minor, but signals the suite grew by copy-paste.

opportunities: Promote a hardened typing-sync test to the required suite: use pressSequentially with delays and poll the peer's draft-indicator textContent, asserting at least N distinct intermediate values (e.g. 'H', 'Hel', 'Hello') — this is the only way to actually guard the product's core feature. The existing waitForPeersConnected + strict-mode plumbing already supports it.

Replace waitForP2PSync sleeps with expect.poll / locator-based waits, and add a test-mode env flag to signaling-server.js that disables the per-IP rate limit so throttleRoomNavigation (test-utils.ts:15-24) can be deleted — likely halves suite wall-clock time and removes the biggest flake source, which would let most best-effort tests graduate to required.

Delete or merge the can't-fail tests (peer-list.spec.ts:180-214, mobile-p2p.spec.ts:196-215, the duplicated offline tests) and regenerate TESTING.md from the actual spec list — the doc is currently a liability.

Add a required test that kills and restarts the signaling server mid-session (the orchestration script already owns the process) to cover Disconnected → Reconnecting → Connected and the reconnect button — the most common real-world reliability path, currently untested.
Add Rust unit tests for crypto.rs round-trip (encrypt/decrypt, wrong-key failure, IV uniqueness) — cargo test already runs in verify:rust so it's free CI.

For mobile: drop the maxTouchPoints=0 override on Mobile projects and instead implement+test the touch interaction path (tap/long-press for reactions/edit). Until the app has one, the Mobile Safari/Chrome projects mostly re-run desktop tests or skip.

File/media transfer test infrastructure (for the planned feature): (1) binary fixtures (a few KB image, ~5MB video) checked into tests/fixtures, sent via Playwright setInputFiles, verified by hashing received bytes in page.evaluate — hash-equality is the core assertion; (2) extend the WebSocket.send interception harness from required-network.spec.ts:19-42 to assert file bytes NEVER appear in relay frames (the 'no server relay for media' product requirement is exactly testable this way); (3) add an analogous RTCDataChannel.prototype.send shim to assert chunked + encrypted payloads on the P2P path — this also fixes the existing DataChannel-encryption blind spot for text; (4) CDP Network.emulateNetworkConditions throughput throttling (pattern already exists at required-network.spec.ts:119-125) to test chunking/backpressure/progress UI and transfer cancellation on slow links; (5) mid-transfer disconnect/resume test reusing the cleanup(contextB)/rejoin pattern from required-p2p.spec.ts:46-83; (6) for audio/video capture, Chromium launch args --use-fake-device-for-media-stream/--use-fake-ui-for-media-stream as a new Playwright project, since the current projects can't grant camera/mic; (7) persistence tests must move beyond localStorage assertions — media will need IndexedDB, so persistence.spec.ts's key-existence pattern won't transfer.
result.verdict: Keep and refactor — do not rewrite. The bones are genuinely good: real multi-context WebRTC E2E, a blocking/non-blocking split with strict mode, a clever wire-level encryption assertion, solid orchestration scripting, and a staging matrix with soak testing. That's more test infrastructure than most P2P side projects ever build. But the suite currently protects the wrong things: the required suite guards solo-browser UI and two narrow P2P paths while the product's defining feature (live typing sync), all touch/mobile interactions, primary-path encryption, and reconnection UX sit in a skippable, non-blocking, sleep-riddled best-effort tier that can pass while executing nothing. The refactor priorities are mechanical, not architectural: remove the signaling rate-limit conflict so soft-skips can become hard requirements, promote typing-sync (with a real progressive-content assertion) into the required suite, stop forcing fastConnect and desktop touch detection everywhere, and delete the tautological tests. Doing that before building file/media transfer matters doubly, because the transfer features will need the same harness (DataChannel inspection, relay-frame assertions, disconnect/resume) that the current suite half-built for text.

result.summary:
Mindline advertises "E2E encrypted P2P chat with AES-256-GCM per-room keys." I traced the full key lifecycle and the actual message send/receive path. The advertised encryption is almost entirely dead code, and the live confidentiality story is very different from the marketing.

Key birth and distribution: The "room key" is born in Rust as 32 random bytes per device (src-rust/crypto.rs:70-91, generate_room_key), generated lazily on room entry (initialize_room_encryption, crypto_api.rs:43-71). It is stored ONLY in localStorage (crypto.rs:243-265) and is NEVER transmitted to any peer — there is no key exchange, no URL fragment, no derivation from the room ID. I grepped the entire src/ + tests: there is no fragment/hash key sharing, and encrypt_message_content / encryptMessageContent (crypto_api.rs:76, wasm/index.ts:294) are never called by any code path. Each participant therefore holds a different, unshared random key that is also never used. The send path (routes/[roomId]/+page.svelte:122 handleSend → manager.ts:240-267 broadcastChat) builds a ChatMessage carrying raw plaintext content with no `encrypted` flag; the receive path only decrypts `if (encrypted ...)` (handlers.ts:98-108), which is never true. So chat messages cross the wire as plaintext JSON.

What actually protects traffic: Direct P2P messages are protected solely by WebRTC's transport DTLS. The only live application-layer crypto is the relay-fallback path: when direct P2P fails, peers do an ephemeral ECDH (P-256) handshake (connection.ts:111-198) and AES-256-GCM-encrypt relay payloads with a fresh random 96-bit IV per message (connection.ts:224-274) before the signaling server forwards them. That construction is sound against a PASSIVE server, but the ECDH public keys are raw, unsigned and relayed by the server itself (connection.ts:139-150, signaling-server.js:330), and the WebRTC DTLS fingerprints travel in SDP through that same server (offer/answer relay, connection.ts:663-669, signaling-server.js:327). There is no out-of-band fingerprint or SAS verification anywhere, so a malicious/compromised signaling server can transparently MITM both the DTLS channel and the relay ECDH.

What the server and a network observer see: The room ID is a UUIDv4 (utils.rs:10, ~122 bits from a CSPRNG) placed in the URL PATH and sent verbatim to the signaling server in the plaintext `join` message (connection.ts:351-357); the server uses it as the room map key and logs it (signaling-server.js:324). A passive network observer with TLS/WSS in production sees only ciphertext. The server operator sees every room ID, the full peer mesh, and — because there is no key/fingerprint authentication — can read or alter all content by MITM. Sync history is sent unencrypted to any peer that asks (handlers.ts:204-211, encrypted:false), so anyone who joins gets the full plaintext backlog.
strengths: Relay-fallback subsystem is well-built: per-connection ephemeral ECDH (P-256) + AES-256-GCM with a fresh crypto.getRandomValues 96-bit IV per message (connection.ts:111-274). This genuinely hides relayed content from a PASSIVE signaling server and gives per-session forward secrecy for relay traffic.
All primitives use Web Crypto SubtleCrypto, not hand-rolled crypto (src/lib/wasm/crypto.ts); AES-GCM tagLength explicitly 128.
Room IDs are UUIDv4 sourced from a CSPRNG (getrandom 'js' feature + crypto.getRandomValues, Cargo.toml + utils.rs:10) — ~122 bits of entropy, not guessable.

Hardened signaling server assigns server-side client IDs to stop transport-level peer-ID spoofing, and adds rate limiting, 16KB payload caps, and a strict CORS allowlist (signaling-server.js:196-223, 30-35, 510-525).
Production uses WSS/TLS for signaling (config.ts useSSL), protecting the discovery channel and relay frames from passive network observers in transit.

issues.title: Advertised AES-256-GCM room-key encryption is never applied to messages — chat is broadcast in plaintext
issues.detail: handleSend (routes/[roomId]/+page.svelte:122-180) calls broadcastChat (p2p/manager.ts:240-267), which constructs ChatMessage with raw `content` and no `encrypted` flag. handleChatMessage only decrypts when `encrypted` is truthy (p2p/handlers.ts:96-108), which never happens. encrypt_message_content (src-rust/crypto_api.rs:76) and its proxy encryptMessageContent (wasm/index.ts:294) have zero call sites in src/ or tests. Net effect: on the direct-P2P path, message confidentiality is provided ONLY by WebRTC DTLS; the entire src-rust/crypto.rs room-key module is dead code in the live path. The 'AES-256-GCM per-room key E2E' claim is false as shipped.

issues.title: Per-room key is generated independently per device and never shared — architecturally cannot decrypt peers
issues.detail: generate_room_key (src-rust/crypto.rs:70-91) fills 32 random bytes via crypto.getRandomValues and stores the key only in localStorage (crypto.rs:243-265). No code transmits the key to peers: no key exchange message type, no URL fragment, no derivation from room ID (verified by grep across src/). Two participants in the same room therefore hold different keys. Even if encryption were wired into the send path, peer B could never decrypt peer A's ciphertext. The 'shared room key' does not exist as a shared secret.

issues.title: No authentication of DTLS fingerprints or ECDH keys — malicious signaling server can MITM both transport and relay
issues.detail: WebRTC SDP offers/answers (containing DTLS fingerprints) are relayed by the server (connection.ts:663-669; signaling-server.js:327-345). Relay ECDH public keys are raw, unsigned, and relayed by the same server (connection.ts:139-150 send; connection.ts:167-198 accept; signaling-server.js:330). There is no out-of-band fingerprint comparison, SAS, or binding of key material to the room secret anywhere. A compromised/hostile signaling server can substitute its own DTLS fingerprint and its own ECDH key in each direction, terminating both channels and reading/altering all content. This defeats E2E against the exact adversary E2E is meant to stop (the server operator).

issues.title: Room ID is the sole access control, is secret-equivalent, yet is sent to and logged by the server; anyone with it reads everything
issues.detail: Room ID (UUIDv4) lives in the URL path (+page.svelte:48) and is sent in cleartext in the `join` frame (connection.ts:351-357). The signaling server uses it as the room key and logs it (signaling-server.js:324 `joined room ${currentRoom}`). There is no join authentication: any party holding the room ID can join, receive the peer list, establish data channels, and obtain all plaintext messages plus a full unencrypted history sync. The room secret is thus simultaneously the routing key the server sees AND the only capability gate — they should be separated.

issues.title: Application-level sender identity is self-asserted and forgeable; edit/delete ownership checks are trivially bypassed
issues.detail: senderId/senderName are attacker-controlled fields set by the sender (manager.ts:255-263) and trusted on receipt (handlers.ts:111-114). The edit and delete 'ownership' checks compare the incoming senderId to the stored message's sender_id (handlers.ts:377, 417) — both supplied by the attacker — so a peer can impersonate any user and forge, edit, or delete messages as anyone in the room. No message signing exists.

issues.title: Full room history is synced unencrypted to any peer on request
issues.detail: handleSyncRequest returns all room messages with encrypted:false (handlers.ts:204-211), comment explicitly notes 'peers may have different room keys (key sharing not yet implemented).' Any joiner — or a MITM server — obtains the complete plaintext backlog on connect.

issues.title: integrity_hash is an unkeyed SHA-256 — security theater that implies integrity it does not provide
issues.detail: EncryptedData carries integrity_hash = SHA256(hex(ciphertext)+hex(nonce)) (src-rust/crypto.rs:128-129, 216-222, 376-380). It is unkeyed, so any party (including a MITM) can recompute it after tampering; it adds nothing over GCM's auth tag and misleads reviewers into thinking extra integrity exists. Dead path, but should be removed to avoid false assurance.

issues.title: No key rotation / forward secrecy for room key; message history stored at rest in plaintext
issues.detail: The room key never rotates and persists indefinitely in localStorage (crypto.rs:243-265). save_room_to_storage writes message content verbatim into chatHistory_<room> (src-rust/messages.rs:524-577) with no encryption, despite StoredMessage carrying an `encrypted` bool (storage.rs:17). Anyone with device/localStorage access reads all history; the persisted `encrypted` flag is misleading.

issues.title: Duplicate insecure signaling server in repo trusts client-supplied IDs and uses wildcard CORS
issues.detail: signaling-server/server.js is a second, divergent implementation that takes clientId from the client (line 45), sets Access-Control-Allow-Origin '*' (line 18), and has no rate limiting or payload caps. If this variant is ever deployed instead of the hardened signaling-server.js, peer-ID spoofing and cross-origin abuse become trivial. Dead/duplicate code that should be deleted to prevent misdeployment.

opportunities: Move the room secret into the URL FRAGMENT (after #) so it never reaches the server. Derive the signaling/routing room ID = SHA-256(secret) and the content key = HKDF(secret). This cleanly separates 'capability to join routing' from 'capability to decrypt', and keeps the key off the server entirely — the single highest-leverage change.

Actually wire the derived key into the send path: encrypt every chat/typing/edit/delete/reaction/sync payload before broadcast (reuse encrypt_message_content, currently dead), and decrypt symmetrically. Only then does a 'room key' mean anything.

Authenticate the channel to stop the malicious-server MITM: verify WebRTC DTLS fingerprints and ECDH public keys against the shared room secret (e.g., HMAC them with HKDF(secret)) or surface a Short Authentication String for out-of-band comparison. Without this, no amount of payload encryption yields true E2E.
Sign application messages (or derive an authenticated per-sender key from the shared secret) to eliminate in-room impersonation and forged edits/deletes.

Delete the dead room-key AES module and the unkeyed integrity_hash, and remove the duplicate insecure signaling-server/server.js, to shrink the crypto surface and stop the code from implying guarantees it does not deliver.
Encrypt localStorage history at rest under the room/content key, or document explicitly that local history is plaintext.
result.verdict: Refactor the crypto design before making any E2E claim; the current 'E2E encrypted with per-room AES-256-GCM' marketing is not defensible. Concretely: (1) against a passive network observer the app is reasonably private today — direct messages ride WebRTC DTLS, signaling/relay ride WSS, and the relay path adds genuine ephemeral-ECDH + AES-GCM. (2) Against the signaling server / hosting operator — the threat model E2E exists to address — it provides essentially no protection: the advertised room-key encryption is dead code (chat is broadcast in plaintext), the room key is per-device and never shared so it could not work anyway, sender identity is forgeable, full history syncs in plaintext, and there is zero key/fingerprint authentication so the server can transparently MITM both DTLS and the relay. Keep and build on the relay ECDH subsystem (connection.ts:111-274) — it is the one correct piece. The minimum bar for a defensible E2E claim is: room secret in the URL fragment (off the server) → HKDF content key → encrypt all payloads → authenticate DTLS fingerprints / ECDH keys against that secret (or SAS) → sign or key-bind sender identity. Until those land, market it as 'private P2P chat (transport-encrypted)', not 'end-to-end encrypted'.

result.summary:
Mindline is a no-account, link-first P2P chat web app: SvelteKit UI + a Rust/WASM core, WebRTC DataChannel mesh for transport, and a Node WebSocket signaling server for peer discovery with an encrypted relay fallback. The signature feature works as advertised mechanically: every keystroke is broadcast to peers (100ms debounce, /Users/jakob/Code/lawzava/mindline/src/routes/[roomId]/+page.svelte:182-193) and rendered as a live draft line (DraftIndicator.svelte) that fades after 3s of inactivity (src/lib/stores/drafts.ts:16). Rooms are UUIDs in the URL; identity is auto-generated ("Anonymous" + UUID in localStorage, src/lib/stores/user.ts); history persists per-room in localStorage via the WASM layer with multi-tab merge (src-rust/messages.rs:524-580) and syncs between peers on reconnect (src/lib/p2p/handlers.ts:192-343).

Feature inventory today: create/join room via link or pasted ID (src/routes/+page.svelte:12-70); live character-by-character drafts; text messages (Enter to send) with per-peer delivery ACKs and check/double-check ticks (src/lib/stores/delivery.ts, MessageBubble.svelte:181-219); edit and soft-delete own messages with P2P broadcast and spoofable ownership check (handlers.ts:366-439); emoji reactions from a fixed set of 8 (EmojiPicker.svelte:16) with toggle and merge-on-sync; peer list popover with names; connection status badge with reconnect button, countdown, and sync progress (ConnectionStatus.svelte); message sync on peer connect (last-100 window); exponential-backoff reconnection plus mobile visibility/network-change handlers (src/lib/p2p/manager.ts:418-638); full-mesh WebRTC with deterministic initiator, offer-collision rollback, ICE restart and mesh monitoring (src/lib/p2p/connection.ts); STUN + free openrelay TURN + WebSocket relay fallback encrypted with per-peer ECDH/AES-GCM (connection.ts:111-274); strictDirect mode; Web Share/clipboard room sharing (Header.svelte:23-73); dark/light theme; leave-room confirm dialog; PWA manifest (no service worker); hardened signaling server with rate limits, 16KB payload cap, room caps, and keepalive (signaling-server.js).

The headline gap is encryption. The README/PRIVACY promise "all messages encrypted with 256-bit keys," "no key transmission," and "encrypted message history" (README.md:30, 225, 253; PRIVACY.md:78-81). In reality: each browser generates its own per-room AES key that is never shared and never used on the send path — wasm.encryptMessageContent is called nowhere (verified by grep); broadcastChat sends plaintext over the DataChannel (src/lib/p2p/manager.ts:255-266); sync responses are sent with `encrypted: false` and an explicit comment "key sharing not yet implemented" (handlers.ts:203-211); history is stored as plaintext JSON in localStorage with the unused key sitting beside it (messages.rs:531-576, crypto.rs:243-265). The receive-side decrypt path would not even work — decrypt_message_content returns a Promise (src-rust/crypto_api.rs:97) consumed synchronously (handlers.ts:98-107). Security today is WebRTC DTLS transport encryption plus an unauthenticated ECDH relay channel that the signaling server could MITM. Other promise/code gaps: manifest shortcuts (/?action=create|random), share_target, and the chat-status widget are dead (landing page handles no query params; widgets/chat-status is hardcoded mock JSON); no service worker so "works offline" and installable-PWA claims are unmet; README links to nonexistent SECURITY.md/CHANGELOG.md (README.md:291,295); PRIVACY.md cites a "clear history feature" that doesn't exist (PRIVACY.md:56, grep-verified) and wrong source paths (PRIVACY.md:131-132); room-ID rules (min 8 chars, README.md:246) are never enforced client-side (validateRoomId never called) and the server accepts ≥4 chars (signaling-server.js:273); CLAUDE.md says the server "does NOT relay messages" while signaling-server.js:347-384 does exactly that. Conversely the README roadmap still lists edit/delete and reactions as "planned" though they are shipped.

Onboarding is genuinely strong: host opens URL (~1-2s WASM load gate, AppShell.svelte:16-35), taps "Create New Room" (1 tap), taps Share (1 tap) plus share-sheet taps; guest taps the link (1 tap) and is auto-joined with auto-identity — no name prompt, no account. The long pole is the deliberately staggered P2P handshake (500-1500ms waits per peer, connection.ts:437-501, plus ICE), giving ~5-10s desktop-to-desktop and ~15-30s phone-to-phone before "Connected"; sync fires 1s after connect (manager.ts:97). Friction points: both users default to "Anonymous" (name edit is a small header input nobody will find); the in-room copy button copies the bare UUID instead of the URL ([roomId]/+page.svelte:195-199) while Share copies the URL — inconsistent; on P2P failure the app silently drops into "local mode" with only a toast, and messages typed there never deliver; free openrelay TURN credentials (config.ts:11-22) are a known-unreliable dependency for the worst-case NAT path.
strengths: Zero-friction onboarding is real: no account, no name prompt, auto-identity, 1 tap to create, 1 tap for a guest to join from a link (src/routes/+page.svelte:35-70, AppShell.svelte:16-35) — this is the product's best asset.

The live-draft feature actually works end-to-end and is well-executed: 100ms debounced keystroke broadcast, named draft rows, typing dots for empty drafts, fade-out animation ([roomId]/+page.svelte:182-193, DraftIndicator.svelte, drafts.ts).

Serious connection-reliability engineering: deterministic initiator election with server-assigned IDs, offer-collision rollback with retry queues, ICE restart with backoff, mesh monitoring, mobile visibility/network handlers, exponential-backoff reconnect with visible countdown (connection.ts, manager.ts:418-638, ConnectionStatus.svelte).

Layered transport fallback (direct P2P → TURN → encrypted WebSocket relay) means chat usually works even behind hostile NATs, and the relay path at least has real per-peer ECDH/AES-GCM encryption (connection.ts:111-274).

Mobile UX details are above average for a side project: 44px touch targets, safe-area insets, enterkeyhint=send, no keyboard-popping autofocus on touch, long-press action menu (MessageInput.svelte:37-62, MessageBubble.svelte:110-168).
Per-peer delivery acknowledgments with honest check/double-check semantics and tooltips ('Delivered to 1/2 peers') — rare honesty for a P2P app (delivery.ts, MessageBubble.svelte:194-216).

The signaling server is production-hardened: per-client rate limits, payload caps, room/connection caps, LRU cleanup, keepalive with missed-pong tolerance, spoofing prevention via server-assigned IDs (signaling-server.js:22-50, 347-384, 448-462).
Extensive E2E test surface (15 Playwright specs incl. strict-P2P, mobile, soak tests) aligned with the 'ultra-reliable' goal (package.json:19-30).

issues.title: E2E encryption is advertised but not implemented on any message path
issues.detail: broadcastChat sends plaintext content over the DataChannel; wasm.encryptMessageContent is never called anywhere in src/ (grep-verified). Sync responses are explicitly sent with encrypted:false and the comment 'peers may have different room keys (key sharing not yet implemented)' (src/lib/p2p/handlers.ts:203-211). The per-room AES-256 key generated in crypto_api.rs:43-71 encrypts nothing. README.md:30/225 and PRIVACY.md:78-81 ('All messages encrypted with AES-256', 'keys never leave your device') are false as user-meaningful claims — only DTLS transport encryption protects direct traffic. For a product whose entire privacy story is 'protected by mathematics, not promises' (PRIVACY.md:123), this is the gap to fix or the claim to retract.

issues.title: Chat history stored as plaintext in localStorage, key stored beside it
issues.detail: save_room_to_storage serializes full message content as plaintext JSON under chatHistory_<roomId>; the (unused) room key is saved in the same localStorage under mindline_encryption_key_<roomId> (crypto.rs:252-261). README.md:253 claims 'Encrypted message history'. Any XSS, extension, or shared device reads everything.

issues.title: Relay encryption key exchange is unauthenticated — signaling server can MITM
issues.detail: ECDH public keys are exchanged via the signaling server (sendRelayKey → 'relay-key' message) with no fingerprint verification or binding to room secret. A malicious/compromised server can substitute keys and read all relay-fallback traffic, contradicting PRIVACY.md:38 'It never sees plaintext message content'. Plaintext relay payloads from 'older clients' are also still accepted (connection.ts:560-561).

issues.title: No room access control: anyone with the room ID gets full history; edits/deletes are spoofable
issues.detail: handleSyncRequest sends the entire local message history to any peer that joins the room — the room ID in the URL is the only secret, and the server accepts IDs as short as 4 chars (signaling-server.js:273) while client-side validateRoomId is never called. Edit/delete ownership checks compare against attacker-controlled senderId in the payload (handlers.ts:377, 417), so any peer can edit or delete anyone's messages by claiming the victim's sender_id. TERMS.md:38 acknowledges 'anyone with a room ID can join' but the README's security posture doesn't.

issues.title: Receive-side decryption path is broken (latent bug proving E2EE was never exercised)
issues.detail: wasm.decryptMessageContent returns a js_sys::Promise (src-rust/crypto_api.rs:97-111) but handlers.ts consumes it synchronously (`const result = wasm.decryptMessageContent(content); if (result) decryptedContent = result;`). If any peer ever set encrypted:true, the UI would render '[object Promise]'. Dead code today; will bite the moment key sharing is implemented.

issues.title: Dead PWA surface: manifest shortcuts, share_target, widgets, and no service worker
issues.detail: Shortcuts point to /?action=random and /?action=create, and share_target sends GET params to / — the landing page handles no query params at all (src/routes/+page.svelte:35-76). widgets/chat-status is hardcoded mock data ('general', 3 active users, 'Hello everyone!'). No service worker exists anywhere (grep-verified), so the app does not load offline despite README.md:231 'works offline with cached messages', and install prompts are degraded.

issues.title: Triple state management (Svelte stores + WASM MessageManager + localStorage) with duplicated fallback writes
issues.detail: Every mutation is performed three times: optimistic Svelte store update, WASM state sync (preceded by a defensive loadRoomMessagesFromStorage), then saveRoomMessagesToStorage — and when WASM fails, hand-rolled localStorage JSON surgery is duplicated inline for both edit (236-258) and delete (293-313). The Rust side re-implements the same merge logic (messages.rs:524-580). This is the main source of complexity and race risk in the codebase and delivers no user value over a single TS store + IndexedDB.

issues.title: ~1,900 lines of effectively dead Rust shipped in the WASM bundle
issues.detail: logger.rs (701 lines) + logging_api.rs (220) + performance.rs (343) + advanced_api.rs (150) + sanitizer.rs (283) + storage.rs (154, an 'IndexedDBManager' that is actually an in-memory HashMap with a comment 'In production, this would use IndexedDB', storage.rs:42-44) are unused or barely used from the app. core.rs also retains deprecated send_message/get_messages/join_room and a vestigial CHAT_MANAGER; create_room_with_id is a no-op that only logs (core.rs:244-253). All of this inflates the WASM payload users download on first visit.

issues.title: Documentation/legal drift across README, PRIVACY, CLAUDE.md
issues.detail: README links to SECURITY.md and CHANGELOG.md which do not exist; PRIVACY.md:56 promises a 'clear history feature' that has no UI (grep-verified); PRIVACY.md:131-132 cites src/crypto.rs and js/webrtc.js (wrong/nonexistent paths); CLAUDE.md states the signaling server 'does NOT relay messages' while signaling-server.js:347-384 implements relay; README roadmap still lists edit/delete and reactions as planned though shipped. For a privacy product, auditors hitting broken claims is reputational damage.

issues.title: P2P-failure 'local mode' silently loses messages
issues.detail: If initializeP2P throws, the app shows one toast and continues; messages composed afterward are persisted locally and broadcast to zero peers with no queue-and-retry on reconnect (broadcastChat just warns 'not ready', manager.ts:244-246). A user can chat into the void. There is no outbox/resend of undelivered messages when peers connect — sync-request only pulls, the delivery store records failure but nothing acts on it.

issues.title: Draft auto-fade contradicts the 'radical transparency' premise and copy
issues.detail: Drafts vanish 3s after the peer pauses typing even though their composed text still sits in their input box — README.md:15 promises 'See exactly what others are typing... nothing is hidden'. Pausing hides the draft; deleting text and rewriting is invisible between debounce windows. Either hold the draft while the input is non-empty (truer to the pitch) or soften the marketing.

issues.title: Copy-room-ID button copies a bare UUID, not the joinable URL
issues.detail: copyRoomId writes only roomId to the clipboard while the header Share button copies the full URL (Header.svelte:27). A recipient pasting a bare UUID must find the join field; the most prominent in-room copy affordance produces the worse artifact. Both should copy the URL.

issues.title: Hardcoded free openrelay TURN credentials undermine the reliability goal
issues.detail: The worst-case NAT path depends on metered.ca's free 'openrelayproject' service, which is rate-limited and frequently down. The WS relay fallback masks it, but then all traffic flows through your signaling server — capacity and privacy implications. Self-hosted coturn or a paid TURN tier is needed before promising 'ultra-reliable'.

issues.title: Every keystroke broadcasts the full draft content — O(len²) bandwidth per message
issues.detail: broadcastTyping sends the entire current draft on each (debounced) input event to every peer, including the relay path when DataChannels are down. Fine for 1:1 text, but it compounds with room size (full mesh) and will collide with the 16KB relay payload cap (signaling-server.js:33) for long drafts. Diffs or capped prefixes would fix it.

opportunities: Sharpest framing: 'A live room, not a messenger.' Mindline's both-peers-online constraint is fatal for an async-chat framing but is a feature for a synchronous one: 'Instant private rooms where you watch each other think. One link. No accounts. No servers storing anything.' Compete with 'jumping on a call', not WhatsApp. Target users: pairs and tiny groups already coordinating in real time — couples/close friends, remote pair-programmers, tutoring/language practice, live interviews/support hand-holding, and privacy-conscious 'burner room' conversations. The live-typing twist is the wedge (it makes text feel like talking); the no-account/no-server story is the retention hook.

Make E2EE real with one design change: put the room key in the URL fragment (mindline.chat/<roomId>#<key>) — fragments never reach the server, the existing share flow distributes it automatically, and the WASM AES-GCM code that already exists finally gets used for wire + storage encryption. This single change makes most of README/PRIVACY true and is dramatically simpler than building key-exchange UI.

Cut the WASM layer or give it a real job. Its current used surface (UUIDs, a message map, localStorage writes, unused crypto) is ~300 lines of TypeScript. Either delete src-rust/ entirely (biggest simplification available — removes the triple-state problem, the load gate, and ~1.9k dead lines) or keep Rust only as the crypto/file-chunking engine once E2EE and file transfer exist. Unify persistence on IndexedDB (needed for media anyway; localStorage's ~5MB cap dies on the first photo).

Bloat-vs-core triage for the live-room framing — Core: create/share/join, live drafts, messages, presence/peer list, reconnect machinery, delivery ticks. Keep-but-cheap: reactions (8 emoji is fine), theme. Question: edit/delete (philosophically opposed to 'no time to craft perfect responses', and currently spoofable — consider removing or limiting to a short undo window); persistent history (for the burner-room audience, ephemeral-by-default with opt-in save is both simpler and more on-brand). Delete: manifest shortcuts/share_target/widgets/adaptive-card, deprecated Rust APIs, fake IndexedDBManager, logger/performance/sanitizer Rust modules.

File sharing (P2P-feasible UX): chunked DataChannel transfer (16-64KB chunks, bufferedAmountLow backpressure, per-chunk index + SHA-256 verification, resume-from-chunk on reconnect). Since both peers must be online, frame it as a 'live handoff': sender sees a real-time progress bar mirrored on the receiver's side, transfer pauses with an honest 'waiting for peer' state, and files transfer only while the bubble shows the peer green. Disable or hard-cap the WS relay path for files (16KB server cap, signaling-server.js:33).

Photo/audio/video messages: photos — input capture + canvas/WebCodecs compression to ~200-500KB, send inline via the same chunking, store blobs in IndexedDB, render with blurhash placeholder; voice notes — MediaRecorder opus/webm (~100KB per 10s) are the easiest big win and fit the 'talking' framing perfectly; video — short clips only (compressed, capped ~10-20MB) over chunked DataChannel; for anything longer, pivot to live streaming (add a media track to the already-established RTCPeerConnection — far easier than file transfer and on-brand for a synchronous product).

Multi-device: identity and history are trapped in one browser's localStorage today. Cheapest credible path: QR/link pairing that joins your second device into the room as a hidden peer and reuses the existing sync-request/response protocol to replicate history device-to-device — no servers, consistent with the privacy story. Pair it with simple export/import as the fallback.

Onboarding polish (the funnel is already ~3 taps): make the room page's primary CTA when alone a giant 'Invite' button (share sheet) instead of the small header icon; prompt for a display name inline in the first draft row ('Typing as Anonymous — tap to set name'); make copy-room-ID copy the full URL; replace the silent 'local mode' with a blocking, honest state ('Couldn't connect — your friend won't see messages. Retry / Diagnose'); and add an outbox that auto-resends undelivered messages when a peer connects.

Honest presence for the both-online constraint: show the invited peer's last-seen/online state prominently, and when alone display 'Your friend isn't here — they'll see nothing until they return while you're online.' Consider an optional, privacy-preserving 'knock' (re-send invite) instead of pretending async delivery exists.
result.verdict: Keep the product, rewrite the core honesty layer, and shrink the stack. The UX shell (onboarding, live drafts, reconnect machinery, mobile polish, signaling server) is genuinely good and worth keeping nearly as-is — it delivers the 'seamless, easy' goal better than most P2P chat attempts. But the trust layer must be rebuilt before this can be marketed: E2EE is advertised everywhere and implemented nowhere (plaintext DataChannel broadcasts, plaintext sync, plaintext localStorage), and the fix — room key in the URL fragment plus actually calling the existing AES-GCM code — is small relative to the claim's importance. Simultaneously, the Rust/WASM layer in its current form is negative-value: it adds a load gate, a triple-state synchronization problem, and ~1.9k dead lines while doing work TypeScript does in 300 lines; either delete it or repurpose it as the crypto/file-transfer engine for the planned media features. Re-frame the product around its constraint ('a live room, not a messenger'), cut the dead PWA/widget surface and probably edit/delete, move persistence to IndexedDB, and the planned file/photo/audio features have a clean, P2P-feasible path.
